title: Noah and the Big Boat
subtitle: A story about trusting God
storyText:
Long ago, there lived a man named Noah. The world in Noah's day had grown full of unkindness, and many people had forgotten the God who made them. But Noah was different. Noah walked with God, and he loved to do what was right. In a world that had turned away, Noah listened.

One day God spoke to Noah. "A great flood is coming," God said, "but I will keep you safe." Then God gave Noah a plan. He was to build an ark—an enormous boat of strong wood, with many rooms inside, a door in its side, and a window up high. God told Noah exactly how long and how wide and how tall to make it, down to the very last measurement.

It was an enormous task. Noah had never built anything so large. But the Bible tells us, in words as simple as they are wonderful: "Thus Noah did. He did all that God commanded him" (Genesis 6:22). Day after day, Noah measured and sawed and hammered. The neighbors may have stared. Some may even have laughed, for there was no flood in sight and no sea nearby. Yet Noah kept building, because he trusted the One who had spoken.

When at last the ark was finished, the animals came. They came walking and waddling, padding and crawling, flying and hopping—two by two, a mother and a father of every kind. Big animals and small ones, furry ones and feathered ones, filed up into the ark and found their places. Noah's wife, his sons, and their wives came inside too. And when everyone was safely in, God Himself closed the door behind them.

Then the rain began.

It fell softly at first, then harder, drumming on the roof of the ark. Water rose over the gardens, over the hills, over the highest places. But inside the ark, Noah and his family were dry and safe. The great boat lifted up and floated, rocking gently on the wide water while the rain sang against the wood. God was watching over every living thing inside.

The rain fell for forty days and forty nights. And then the Bible says something beautiful: "God remembered Noah, all the animals, and all the livestock that were with him in the ship" (Genesis 8:1). God had never forgotten them—not for a single moment. He sent a wind across the water, and slowly, slowly, the flood began to go down.

After many days, the ark came to rest on the top of a mountain. Noah opened the window and sent out a dove to see if the ground was dry. The first time, the little dove found no place to land and flew back. Noah waited and sent her again. This time she returned with a fresh green olive leaf in her beak—new life was growing! When Noah sent her once more, she did not come back at all, and Noah knew it was time.

God told Noah to come out of the ark. Out stepped Noah and his family into the clean, washed world, and out came all the animals behind them, ready to fill the earth once more. The very first thing Noah did was build an altar and thank God for keeping him safe.

Then God gave a gift to Noah and to all the world. He set a rainbow arching across the sky—red and orange, yellow and green, blue and violet—shining after the rain. "I set my rainbow in the cloud," God said, "and it will be a sign of a covenant between me and the earth" (Genesis 9:13). A covenant is a promise that will never be broken. Every rainbow since that day whispers the same gentle truth: God keeps His word.

Sometimes our days can feel like a long rain, or a job too big, or a sky too gray. But Noah's story reminds us that God sees, God remembers, and God is faithful—from the first raindrop to the last.

So tonight, little one, as you close your eyes, remember the rainbow. The same God who watched over Noah through every wave is watching over you right now, and He will keep you all through the night.
takeaway: God keeps His promises and watches over us through every storm.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for loving me and keeping me safe like You kept Noah. When things feel big, help me trust You. Help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "I set my rainbow in the cloud, and it will be a sign of a covenant between me and the earth." — Genesis 9:13
talkAboutIt: God set a rainbow in the sky as a promise He would always keep. What is a promise someone has kept for you?

title: Daniel and the Lions
subtitle: A story about courage and faith
storyText:
In the great city of Babylon there lived a man named Daniel, and Daniel loved God with his whole heart. He had served under kings for many years, and now King Darius ruled the land. The king saw that Daniel was wise and honest and good, and he planned to put Daniel in charge of the whole kingdom.

But some of the other leaders did not like that. They grew jealous of Daniel, and they began to look for some reason to get him in trouble. They watched him all day long. Yet Daniel was so faithful and so fair that they could not find a single wrong thing. "We will never find anything against this Daniel," they finally said, "unless it has to do with the way he worships his God."

So they made a sneaky plan. They went to the king and asked him to sign a new law: for thirty days, no one in all the land could pray to anyone except the king. Anyone who disobeyed would be thrown into the den of lions. King Darius did not know it was a trap, and he signed it.

Now Daniel heard about the new law. He could have closed his window and prayed quietly so no one would know. But Daniel loved God too much to hide. He went home, to the upstairs room where his windows opened toward the city of Jerusalem, and he did just as he had always done — "…he kneeled on his knees three times a day, and prayed, and gave thanks before his God, as he did before" (Daniel 6:10).

The jealous men were watching, of course. They hurried to the king. "Daniel is still praying to his God!" they said. The king was very sad, for he loved Daniel. All day he tried to find a way to save him, but the law could not be changed. At last, with a heavy heart, the king gave the order. And as Daniel was lowered into the den, the king called out something wonderful: "Your God whom you serve continually, he will deliver you" (Daniel 6:16).

That night the king could not eat. He could not sleep. He waited and waited for morning to come. And inside the dark den, Daniel was not afraid, and Daniel was not alone, for God had sent His angel to be with him among the lions.

As soon as the sun rose, the king ran to the den. With a trembling voice he called, "Daniel, servant of the living God, is your God, whom you serve continually, able to deliver you from the lions?" For a moment, all was quiet. And then, out of the darkness, came Daniel's voice, calm and glad: "My God has sent his angel, and has shut the lions' mouths, and they have not hurt me" (Daniel 6:22).

The king was filled with joy! He had Daniel lifted up out of the den, and not even a scratch was found on him, because he had trusted in his God. Then King Darius sent a message to everyone in his whole kingdom, telling them about the God of Daniel — the living God who saves and rescues, whose love lasts forever.

Daniel's story reminds us that we can be brave when we choose to do what is right, even when it feels hard. Daniel kept praying because he knew that talking with God was the best part of his day. And the same God who shut the lions' mouths and watched over Daniel all through that long, dark night is watching over you too.

So tonight, little one, you do not need to be afraid of the dark or anything in it. God is near to you, just as He was near to Daniel. You can close your eyes, say a quiet thank-you to Him, and rest, because the God who never sleeps is keeping you safe until morning.
takeaway: We can be brave and faithful because God stays near us, even in the dark.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for staying close to me the way You stayed close to Daniel. Help me do what is right and be brave, even when things feel scary. Watch over me tonight and help me sleep peacefully. Amen.
memoryVerse: "My God has sent his angel, and has shut the lions’ mouths." — Daniel 6:22
talkAboutIt: Daniel kept praying and thanking God every day, even when it was scary. What is something you can thank God for tonight?

title: Jesus Calms the Storm
subtitle: A story about peace in the middle of fear
storyText:
It had been a long and busy day. All day long, Jesus had been teaching beside the Sea of Galilee, and great crowds had pressed close to hear every word. When evening came and the sky began to soften into dusk, Jesus turned to His friends, the disciples, and said, "Let's go over to the other side of the lake."

So they left the crowd behind and climbed into a little fishing boat. Jesus was so tired from the long day that He went to the back of the boat, rested His head on a cushion, and fell fast asleep. The boat slipped out across the water, and the gentle rocking must have felt like a cradle.

But the Sea of Galilee could change in a hurry. Without warning, a fierce wind came rushing down over the water. The waves grew bigger and bigger. They rose up tall and crashed against the little boat, splashing over the sides, until water began to fill it. The disciples had sailed these waters many times—some of them were fishermen—but this storm frightened even them. They pulled at the ropes and the sails, but the wind was simply too strong.

And through it all, Jesus kept sleeping.

At last the disciples could bear it no longer. They woke Him, crying out, "Teacher, don't You care that we are going to drown?"

Jesus opened His eyes. He stood up in the rocking boat. And then He did something only God could do. He spoke—not to His frightened friends, but to the wind and the waves themselves. "Peace! Be still!" He said.

And the wind heard Him.

At once the wind stopped. The waves lay down flat. The Bible says, "The wind ceased, and there was a great calm" (Mark 4:39). In a single moment, the wild storm became as quiet as a sleeping child. The water turned smooth as glass, and the only sound was the soft lapping against the boat.

Jesus turned gently to His friends. "Why are you so afraid?" He asked them. "How is it that you have no faith?" He was teaching them something they would never forget: that He was always near, and that they were never, ever alone—not even in the middle of a storm.

The disciples looked at the calm water, and then at Jesus, and a holy wonder filled their hearts. They whispered to one another, "Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?" They were beginning to understand. The One asleep in their boat was the very One who had made the wind and the sea in the beginning. Of course they obeyed His voice.

Sometimes our hearts can feel like that little boat in the storm—when we are worried, or afraid, or when things feel too big for us. But Jesus is never far away. The same voice that calmed the wind and the waves can calm our hearts and fill us with peace.

So tonight, little one, if any worry tries to follow you to bed, you can remember the boat, and the storm, and the gentle, mighty voice of Jesus. Let His words settle softly over you now: "Peace. Be still." You are safe. You are loved. And the One who calms every storm is watching over you while you sleep.
takeaway: Jesus is always near, and His peace can calm even our biggest worries.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You that You are always with me, even when I feel afraid. Calm my heart the way You calmed the storm, and help me rest. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Peace! Be still!" — Mark 4:39
talkAboutIt: Jesus said, "Peace! Be still!" and the storm grew calm. What is something that worries you that you can give to Jesus tonight?

title: The Lost Sheep
subtitle: A story about how much we are loved
storyText:
One day, some people gathered close around Jesus to hear Him teach. Among them were people whom others looked down on, but Jesus welcomed every one of them. To help them all understand how much God loves each and every person, Jesus told a gentle little story about a shepherd and his sheep.

A shepherd is someone who takes care of sheep. He leads them to soft green grass. He brings them to cool water to drink. He keeps them safe from anything that might hurt them, and at the end of the day he counts them, one by one, to make sure every single sheep is there.

In Jesus' story, the shepherd had one hundred sheep. That is a lot of sheep! But one day, as the shepherd counted, he found that one little sheep was missing. It had wandered off and gotten lost, all on its own, far away from the warm and safe little flock.

The shepherd did not shrug and say, "Oh well, I still have ninety-nine." No — that one lost sheep mattered to him very much. Jesus asked, "Which of you men, if you had one hundred sheep, and lost one of them, wouldn't leave the ninety-nine in the wilderness, and go after the one that was lost, until he found it?" (Luke 15:4).

So the good shepherd left the ninety-nine sheep in a safe place, and he went out to look for the one. Over the hills he searched. Through the bushes and along the rocky paths he searched, calling softly, looking under every branch and behind every rock, never giving up. The sky grew darker, but still he kept on searching, until at last — there it was! His lost little sheep.

And do you know what the shepherd did when he found it? He was not angry. He did not scold the little sheep. The Bible says, "When he has found it, he carries it on his shoulders, rejoicing" (Luke 15:5). He lifted that tired, frightened sheep up onto his strong shoulders, held it close and safe and warm, and carried it all the way home, smiling the whole way.

When he got home, the shepherd was so happy that he called his friends and his neighbors together. "Rejoice with me," he said, "for I have found my sheep which was lost!" Everyone celebrated, because the one who had been lost was now safely found.

Then Jesus told the people what the story really meant. The good shepherd is like God. Each one of us is like a precious little sheep. And just as that shepherd loved the one lost sheep enough to go searching for it, God loves you that much too. No one is too small to matter to God. No one is ever forgotten.

Sometimes we might feel lost, or far away, or all alone. But God never stops loving us, and He never stops looking for us. He comes close. He gathers us up gently, like a shepherd lifting a little lamb, and He carries us home with joy.

So tonight, little one, you can close your eyes and rest, because the Good Shepherd knows your name and loves you with all His heart. He is watching over you right now, holding you safe and close, all through the quiet night.
takeaway: God loves each one of us so much that He gently seeks us out and carries us home.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for being my Good Shepherd who loves me and knows my name. Thank You that I am never lost to You. Hold me close and help me rest safely tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "When he has found it, he carries it on his shoulders, rejoicing." — Luke 15:5
talkAboutIt: The shepherd searched and searched for just one little sheep. How does it feel to know God would come looking for you?

title: The Birth of Jesus
subtitle: A story about God’s greatest gift
storyText:
Long ago, in a little town called Nazareth, there lived a young woman named Mary. God had chosen Mary for the most special part in the most wonderful plan the world has ever known. An angel had told her that she would have a baby, and that this baby would be the Son of God. His name would be Jesus.

Around that time, the ruler of the land said that everyone must travel back to the town their family came from, to have their names written down. So Mary and a kind man named Joseph set out on a long journey to the town of Bethlehem. It was far away, and the road was dusty and slow, and by the time they finally arrived, the night had grown dark and Mary was very tired.

Bethlehem was crowded with people who had come for the very same reason. Mary and Joseph looked for a room where they could rest, but every place was full. There was no room for them in the inn. At last, they found a quiet little shelter where the animals were kept. It was simple and warm, and there, in the soft hush of the night, baby Jesus was born.

The Bible tells it gently and simply: "She wrapped him in bands of cloth, and laid him in a feeding trough, because there was no room for them in the inn" (Luke 2:7). The King of all the world had come, not to a grand palace, but to a humble manger, where He was loved and held and kept warm.

Out in the fields nearby, some shepherds were watching over their sheep in the dark. All at once, an angel of the Lord appeared, and the bright glory of God shone all around them. The shepherds were afraid, but the angel said something beautiful: "Don't be afraid, for behold, I bring you good news of great joy which will be to all the people" (Luke 2:10). A Savior had been born for them — the One they had waited so long for — and they would find Him wrapped snugly and lying in a manger.

Then the whole sky filled with angels, more than anyone could count, all praising God and singing, "Glory to God in the highest, on earth peace, good will toward men" (Luke 2:14). The night that had been so quiet was suddenly full of heaven's song.

When the angels had gone, the shepherds hurried off to Bethlehem to see. And there they found everything just as the angel had said: Mary and Joseph, and the little baby lying in the manger. The shepherds knelt down with wonder in their hearts, and then they went out telling everyone the good news. Mary kept all these things and thought about them, treasuring them deep in her heart.

This is why the birth of Jesus is such happy, hopeful news. God did not stay far away in heaven. He came down close to be with us, as a tiny baby who needed to be rocked and fed and loved, just like you once were. He came to bring light into the dark, and peace to the whole world, and love that would never, ever end.

So tonight, little one, as the house grows still and your eyes start to feel heavy, you can remember that quiet, holy night in Bethlehem. The same God who sent His greatest gift, baby Jesus, into the world is right here with you. He came near, and He stays near, watching over you with love as you drift off gently to sleep.
takeaway: Jesus is God's greatest gift, who came close to bring us love, hope, and peace.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for sending baby Jesus, Your greatest gift. Thank You that You came near to be with us and that You are near to me right now. Help me rest in Your love and peace tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "For there is born to you today, in David’s city, a Savior, who is Christ the Lord." — Luke 2:11
talkAboutIt: Baby Jesus was born in a humble little stable, not a fancy palace. Why do you think that good news made the shepherds so happy?

title: David and Goliath
subtitle: A story about brave faith
storyText:
In the days long ago, two armies stood facing each other across a wide valley. On one side were the people of Israel, God's people. On the other side were the Philistines. And the Philistines had a champion — a giant of a man named Goliath. He was taller than the tallest man, with heavy armor that gleamed in the sun, and every single day he stomped out and shouted at Israel, daring someone, anyone, to come and fight him. The soldiers of Israel took one look at that giant and trembled with fear.

Now there was a young shepherd boy named David. He was the youngest of his family, too young even to be a soldier. One morning his father sent him to the army camp to bring food to his older brothers and to see how they were doing. When David arrived, he heard the giant booming out his challenge, and he saw how frightened everyone was.

But David was not afraid, because David knew something the others had forgotten. While the soldiers were busy looking at how big the giant was, David was thinking about how great God is. Out in the fields, watching his sheep, David had trusted God to help him when a lion and a bear had come — and God had kept him safe. So David said he would go and face the giant himself.

The king tried to dress David in heavy armor, but it was far too big and clunky for a shepherd boy. So David took it off. Instead, he picked up his shepherd's staff, his sling, and five smooth stones from a stream, and he walked out to meet Goliath with nothing but those — and his great trust in God.

When Goliath saw a young boy coming toward him, he laughed. But David called out with a brave and steady voice: "You come to me with a sword, with a spear, and with a javelin; but I come to you in the name of the LORD of Armies, the God of the armies of Israel" (1 Samuel 17:45). David knew that this fight did not really belong to him at all. "…the battle is the LORD's," he said, "and he will give you into our hand" (1 Samuel 17:47).

Then David ran toward the giant. He reached into his bag, set a stone in his sling, and swung it round and round. The little stone flew through the air, straight and true, and struck the giant on the forehead. And down came Goliath, falling to the ground with a great thud. Just like that, the battle was over, and God had given His people the victory — through one brave shepherd boy and one small stone.

When the people of Israel saw it, they cheered. The thing they had been so afraid of was finished, because David had trusted in a God far bigger than any giant.

David's story reminds us that the scary things in life do not get to have the last word. We do not have to be the biggest, the strongest, the tallest, or the oldest for God to use us. We only have to trust Him. God loves to help small people do brave and mighty things, when they put their hand in His.

So tonight, little one, if anything feels too big — a worry, a fear, or a problem that seems as tall as a giant — you can remember David and his sling and his five smooth stones. The same God who was with David is right here with you. He is greater than every giant, and He is watching over you with love as you close your eyes and rest.
takeaway: We can be brave with God's help, because He is greater than any giant we face.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that You are bigger than anything that scares me. When something feels too big, help me trust You like David did. Give me courage and a peaceful, restful sleep tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "…the battle is the LORD’s, and he will give you into our hand." — 1 Samuel 17:47
talkAboutIt: David trusted God instead of looking at how big the giant was. What is something big you can ask God to help you be brave about?

title: Jonah and the Big Fish
subtitle: A story about listening to God
storyText:
One day, God spoke to a man named Jonah. God had an important job for him. "Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city," God said, "and preach against it" (Jonah 1:2). The people of the big city of Nineveh had been doing many wrong things, and God wanted Jonah to go and tell them to turn back and do what is right.

But Jonah did not want to go. Instead of obeying God, Jonah did something silly — he tried to run away! He went down to the sea, found a ship sailing far away in the opposite direction, paid his fare, and climbed aboard, hoping to get away from the job God had given him. But you cannot really run away from God, because God is everywhere, and His love goes with us no matter how far we travel.

While Jonah was sailing away, a great storm came. The wind howled and the waves grew tall, and the little ship was tossed up and down, up and down. The sailors were frightened. But Jonah knew the truth deep in his heart: the trouble had come because he was running away from God. So Jonah told the sailors to put him into the sea, and when they did, the wind stopped and the water grew calm and quiet again.

Now Jonah was in the deep water all alone — but God had not forgotten him. God loved Jonah, and God sent a great big fish to swallow Jonah up and keep him safe. There, in the dark belly of the fish, Jonah was warm and protected, like being tucked inside a great quiet shelter in the middle of the sea.

And what did Jonah do inside that fish? He prayed. He talked to God, and he was sorry for running away. He remembered how good and how kind God is. "I called because of my affliction to the LORD," Jonah prayed. "He answered me… You heard my voice" (Jonah 2:2). God heard Jonah's prayer, the way He hears every prayer.

After three days, God told the fish to bring Jonah safely to dry land. And gently, the great fish carried Jonah right up onto the shore. Jonah was safe and sound, with another chance to do what God had asked.

This time, when God said, "Arise, go to Nineveh," Jonah went. He walked all the way to the great city and told the people God's message. And do you know what? The people listened! They were sorry, and they turned back to God, and God was so glad to forgive them. For God is kind and patient, slow to get angry, and full of loving care for everyone.

Jonah's story reminds us that God gives second chances. Even when we make a wrong choice, even when we try to run the other way, God does not stop loving us. He stays close. He listens when we talk to Him. And He gently leads us back to the right path, like a loving parent reaching out a hand.

So tonight, little one, you can rest knowing that God is patient and kind, and that He always hears you when you pray — whether you are happy or sad, big or small, near or far away. There is nowhere you could ever go that God's love cannot reach. Close your eyes now, and rest in His tender care all through the night.
takeaway: God gives second chances and always hears us when we pray to Him.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for being patient and kind, and for always hearing me when I pray. Help me listen to You and follow where You lead. Thank You that Your love can reach me anywhere. Help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "I called because of my affliction to the LORD. He answered me." — Jonah 2:2
talkAboutIt: Even inside the big fish, Jonah talked to God and God heard him. What would you like to talk to God about tonight?

title: Baby Moses
subtitle: A story about God’s protection
storyText:
Long, long ago, in the land of Egypt, there lived a mother who loved her brand-new baby boy with all her heart. He was tiny and soft and perfect, and she wanted more than anything to keep him safe.

But it was a hard time for her family and her people. The king of Egypt, called Pharaoh, had made a cruel rule, and it was not safe for baby boys. So the mother kept her little one hidden close and quiet for as long as she could. And all the while, she trusted God to watch over her precious baby.

When the baby grew too big to hide any longer, his mother had a gentle idea. The Bible tells us how, "…she took a papyrus basket for him, and coated it with tar and with pitch. She put the child in it, and laid it in the reeds by the river's bank" (Exodus 2:3). She made the little basket like a tiny floating cradle, soft inside and water-tight outside, so it would stay dry and safe. Then she carefully set it among the tall green reeds at the edge of the great river, and she trusted him into God's loving care.

The baby's big sister, named Miriam, hid nearby to watch over him. She wanted to see what would happen to her little brother.

Soon, someone came down to the river — it was Pharaoh's own daughter, the princess of Egypt! She had come to bathe in the cool water, and there among the reeds she noticed the little basket. When she opened it and saw the baby, he began to cry, and her heart melted with kindness. She felt sorry for him and wanted to care for him, even though she knew he was one of the Hebrew children.

God was already at work, watching over this little one in ways no one could see. Right then, brave Miriam stepped forward and asked the princess if she would like someone to help take care of the baby. "Yes," said the princess. So Miriam ran and brought — who do you think? — the baby's very own mother! And the princess asked her to care for the baby and even paid her to do it. What a wonderful surprise! The baby was given right back to his own mama's loving arms.

When the boy was a little older, he came to live with the princess, and she gave him a special name. "She named him Moses," the Bible says, "and said, 'Because I drew him out of the water'" (Exodus 2:10). And that little baby Moses would grow up to do great and important things for God and for his people.

From the very first moment, God was watching over baby Moses with tender, gentle love. He used a brave mother, a watchful sister, and a kind princess to keep him safe — and not one of them even knew the whole wonderful plan God was weaving. God saw it all. God was caring for Moses every step of the way.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down to sleep, remember the baby in the floating basket. The same God who watched over baby Moses among the reeds is watching over you right now. You are safe, you are loved, and God is holding you gently in His care all through the quiet night.
takeaway: God watches over us with tender, loving care, even before we know His plans.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for watching over me the way You watched over baby Moses. Thank You for the people who keep me safe and love me. Help me rest tonight knowing You are always near. Amen.
memoryVerse: "She named him Moses, and said, ‘Because I drew him out of the water.’" — Exodus 2:10
talkAboutIt: God used Moses' mother, his sister, and a kind princess to keep him safe. Who are some of the people God uses to keep you safe?

title: The Good Samaritan
subtitle: A story about kindness
storyText:
A long time ago, a teacher of the law came to Jesus with a question. "What must I do to live forever with God?" he asked. Jesus answered him with a question of His own: what did the Scriptures say? And the man knew the answer by heart. "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength, and with all your mind; and your neighbor as yourself" (Luke 10:27).

"You have answered correctly," Jesus told him. But the man wanted to be sure he was doing it right, so he asked one more thing: "And who is my neighbor?" To answer, Jesus told a story.

Once there was a man traveling down the long, rocky road from Jerusalem to Jericho. It was a lonely road, winding between high cliffs. As he walked, robbers came upon him. They took everything he had and hurt him and left him lying by the side of the road, unable to get up.

After a while, a priest came down that same road. He was an important man who served in God's temple. He saw the hurt traveler lying there. But instead of stopping, he crossed to the other side and kept walking.

Soon another man came along, a Levite who also helped in the temple. He too saw the man lying in the dust. And he too passed by on the other side. The poor traveler must have wondered if anyone in all the world would stop to help him.

Then a third man came down the road, riding on his donkey. He was a Samaritan, from a land whose people were not friends with the traveler's people at all. No one would have expected him to help. But the Bible says, "When he saw him, he was moved with compassion" (Luke 10:33). His heart was filled with kindness, and he could not pass by.

The Samaritan knelt down in the dust beside the hurt man. He gently cleaned and bandaged his wounds, pouring on oil and wine to help them heal. Then he lifted the man onto his own donkey and walked beside him all the way to an inn, where he could rest in safety. He cared for him through the night.

The next morning the Samaritan still was not finished being kind. He took out his own money and gave it to the innkeeper. "Take care of him," he said, "and whatever more you spend, I will pay you when I come back." He made sure the stranger would be looked after even after he had gone.

When Jesus finished the story, He turned to the teacher of the law and asked, "Now which of these three do you think seemed to be a neighbor to him who fell among the robbers?" The man knew the answer. "The one who showed mercy on him," he said. And Jesus told him gently, "Go and do likewise" (Luke 10:37).

The priest and the Levite were busy and important, but they hurried past. It was the one no one expected who stopped, and knelt, and helped. A neighbor, Jesus was teaching, is not only the person who lives next door or looks just like us. A neighbor is anyone who needs our kindness, and we can be a neighbor to anyone we meet.

This is how God loves us. When we are hurt or lonely or far from home, He does not pass by on the other side. He comes near, He gently cares for us, and He never stops watching over us. And because He loves us so faithfully, He gives us kind hearts to share that same love with others.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down and the day grows quiet, think of the kind Samaritan who stopped to help. You can be kind like that too. And remember that the God who fills hearts with compassion is watching over you with the tenderest care of all, holding you safe all through the night.
takeaway: God wants us to show mercy and be a kind neighbor to anyone who needs us.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for loving me and always coming near when I need You. Help me have a kind heart that stops to help others. Help me rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength, and with all your mind; and your neighbor as yourself." — Luke 10:27
talkAboutIt: The Samaritan stopped to help when others walked past. Who is someone you could show kindness to, the way the Samaritan did?

title: Creation Story
subtitle: A story about God’s beautiful world
storyText:
In the very, very beginning, before there were stars to twinkle or birds to sing, there was God. The world had no shape yet, and everything was dark and still. And then God began to make something beautiful.

The Bible tells us how it started, in the oldest and gentlest of words: "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth" (Genesis 1:1).

On the first day, God spoke into the darkness. "Let there be light," He said, "and there was light" (Genesis 1:3). Soft, warm light spread across the world, and God saw that the light was good. He called the light "day" and the darkness "night." And so there was a first morning and a first evening, just like the morning and evening you know.

On the second day, God made the great wide sky, stretching high above everything, the place where clouds would one day float by.

On the third day, God gathered the waters together into seas, and dry land appeared. Then He filled the land with green and growing things: soft grass, tall trees, and flowers of every color, with seeds inside to make even more. God looked at it all, and it was good.

On the fourth day, God made the lights in the sky. He set the bright sun to shine in the daytime, and He made the gentle moon and the sparkling stars to glow at night. Now there would be a sun to wake up to and stars to fall asleep beneath.

On the fifth day, God filled the seas with swimming things, from the tiniest silver fish to the great whales that splash in the deep. And He filled the air with birds, every kind that flaps and soars and sings. The world was becoming full of life.

On the sixth day, God made all the animals that walk and run and hop upon the land. Big lumbering elephants and tiny scurrying mice, woolly sheep and leaping deer, and every creature in between. What a wonderful, living world it was becoming.

And then God made something very special. He made people, the very first man and woman, to know Him and love Him and care for His beautiful world. The Bible says, "God created man in his own image. In God's image he created him; male and female he created them" (Genesis 1:27). God formed the man gently from the dust of the ground and breathed life into him, and the man came alive. God loved His people most of all.

When everything was finished, God looked at all He had made, at the light and the sky, the seas and the trees, the sun and moon and stars, the fish and birds and animals, and the people He loved so dearly. And the Bible says it beautifully: "God saw everything that he had made, and, behold, it was very good" (Genesis 1:31).

Then, when all the work of making was done, God rested. He set apart a special day for resting, and it was peaceful and holy. Even God, who never grows tired, showed us how good it is to rest when the day is done.

Everything you can see, the bright moon outside your window, the trees that whisper in the wind, the soft pillow beneath your head, was made by God, who made it all with wisdom and care. And of all the wonderful things He made, He made you too. You are part of His "very good" world.

So tonight, little one, as the sky grows dark and the stars come out to shine, remember that the same God who said "Let there be light" and filled the whole world with beauty is the very same God who made you and loves you. He is watching over you now. Close your eyes and rest, just as God rested, safe and warm in the goodness of all He has made.
takeaway: God made the whole world with wisdom and love, and He made you too.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for making such a beautiful world, and thank You for making me. Help me rest tonight in the goodness of all You have made. Amen.
memoryVerse: "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth." — Genesis 1:1
talkAboutIt: God looked at everything He made and called it very good. What is your favorite thing God made, and why do you love it?

title: Joseph and His Colorful Coat
subtitle: A story about God’s plan
storyText:
Long ago there lived a young man named Joseph, one of twelve brothers. His father, Israel, loved him dearly, and one day he gave Joseph a wonderful gift. The Bible says, "Now Israel loved Joseph more than all his children, because he was the son of his old age, and he made him a coat of many colors" (Genesis 37:3). It was a beautiful coat, bright with every color, and Joseph loved to wear it.

But Joseph's brothers saw the special coat and grew jealous. God had also given Joseph dreams, dreams that hinted he would one day be a leader, and this made his brothers more upset still. Their hearts filled with envy, and one sad day, far out in the fields, they took Joseph's beautiful coat away and sold him to traders traveling to a faraway land called Egypt. Poor Joseph was carried far from his home and his father, all alone.

Yet Joseph was never truly alone. For wherever he went, God went with him. In Egypt, Joseph became a servant in the house of an important man. The Bible tells us something we must never forget: "The LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man" (Genesis 39:2). Joseph worked hard and did what was right, and everything he did went well.

But Joseph's road was not easy. He was treated unfairly and even put in prison for something he did not do. That would be a frightening, lonely place. And still, the Bible says, "the LORD was with Joseph, and showed kindness to him" (Genesis 39:21). Even in prison, God did not leave him. God gave Joseph wisdom to understand dreams, and slowly, in God's good time, things began to change.

One day the king of Egypt, called Pharaoh, had dreams that troubled him and that no one could explain. Someone remembered Joseph, and he was brought up out of the prison. With God's help, Joseph explained the dreams: a great famine was coming, when there would be no food, and the land must store up grain now to be ready. Pharaoh saw how wise Joseph was, and he placed Joseph in charge of all the food in Egypt, the second most important person in the whole land. The young man who had been sold away was now a great leader, just as God had planned.

When the hungry years came, people from many places traveled to Egypt to buy food, and one day Joseph's very own brothers came too. They did not recognize the grand leader before them, but Joseph knew them at once. His heart was full. He could have been angry. Instead, when the moment came, Joseph wept and told them gently, "I am Joseph, your brother... Now don't be grieved, nor angry with yourselves, that you sold me here, for God sent me before you to preserve life" (Genesis 45:4-5).

Joseph forgave his brothers with all his heart. He brought his whole family, including his dear old father, to live safely in Egypt, where there was plenty of food. The family that had been broken was made whole again. Later, Joseph said something wise and beautiful about all the hard things that had happened to him: "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring to pass, as it is today, to save many people alive" (Genesis 50:20).

Joseph's life had many hard parts, sad days and lonely nights. But God was with him through every single one, weaving each moment into a good and loving plan. What others meant for harm, God turned into something that saved many people, even the brothers who had hurt him.

So tonight, little one, if any day feels hard or unfair, remember Joseph. The same God who stayed with him through every step, in the field, in Egypt, even in prison, is with you too, on your happy days and your hard ones. He loves you, He has a good plan for you, and He is watching over you while you sleep.
takeaway: God stays with us through hard times and can turn even sad days into good.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that You are with me on happy days and hard days, just like You were with Joseph. Help me trust Your good plan and rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring to pass, as it is today, to save many people alive." — Genesis 50:20
talkAboutIt: God was with Joseph even when things felt unfair, and brought good from it. Can you think of a hard time when something good came afterward?

title: The Wise Men
subtitle: A story about seeking Jesus
storyText:
Far, far away in a land to the east, there lived wise men who loved to study the night sky. They knew the stars by heart, and they watched them carefully, night after night. Then one night, something wonderful happened. A brand-new star appeared, brighter and more beautiful than any they had ever seen. The wise men knew at once that it was a sign. Somewhere, a great King had been born.

So they packed their camels and began a long, long journey to find Him. They traveled over deserts and hills, following the star, for many days and many nights. At last they came to the city of Jerusalem, and there they began to ask everyone, "Where is he who is born King of the Jews? For we saw his star in the east, and have come to worship him" (Matthew 2:2).

When King Herod heard about this new King, he called for the wise men. The teachers had searched the old Scriptures and found that the special child was to be born in a little town called Bethlehem. So the wise men set out once more, their hearts full of hope.

And there it was again, the star, going ahead of them in the dark sky. It led them gently down the road toward Bethlehem, and then it stopped, shining right over the place where the young child was. The Bible tells us just how the wise men felt in that moment: "When they saw the star, they rejoiced with exceedingly great joy" (Matthew 2:10). After their long, long journey, they had found Him at last.

The wise men went inside, and there was the child Jesus with Mary, His mother. They could hardly believe their eyes. This was the King they had traveled so far to see. And so they did the most wonderful thing. The Bible says, "they fell down and worshiped him. Opening their treasures, they offered to him gifts: gold, frankincense, and myrrh" (Matthew 2:11). These were the finest, most precious treasures they had, and they gave them gladly to the little King.

Imagine it: powerful, learned men who had crossed the whole world, kneeling down before a tiny child, their hearts overflowing with joy and worship. They knew that Jesus was worth every step of the journey and every treasure they could bring.

When it was time to go home, God warned the wise men in a dream not to return to King Herod, who did not have a kind heart. So they went home a different way, carrying the memory of the King they had found, glad they had followed the star all the way to Jesus.

The wise men teach us something gentle and true. Jesus is the greatest treasure in all the world, more precious than gold, worth seeking with all our hearts. The wise men did not know exactly where the journey would lead, but they followed the light God gave them, step by step, and it led them straight to the Savior. When we seek Jesus, we find the One our hearts were made to love.

So tonight, little one, as the sky outside grows dark and the very first stars begin to shine, think of that special star long ago, glowing softly over Bethlehem, leading wise men to the King. Jesus is still the greatest treasure of all, and He loves you dearly. You can seek Him with a joyful heart, knowing He is near, watching over you and keeping you safe all through the night.
takeaway: Jesus is the greatest treasure of all, worth seeking with a joyful heart.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for sending Jesus, the greatest treasure of all. Help me seek Him with a happy heart and rest in His love tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Where is he who is born King of the Jews? For we saw his star in the east, and have come to worship him." — Matthew 2:2
talkAboutIt: The wise men traveled far and gave their finest gifts to Jesus. What gift could you give Jesus from your heart?

title: Christmas: The Birth of Jesus
subtitle: A cozy Christmas bedtime story
storyText:
On a quiet night long ago, in the little town of Bethlehem, something wonderful was about to happen. Mary and Joseph had traveled a long way, and Mary was going to have a very special baby. But when they arrived, every place was full, and there was no room for them to stay. So they found a humble shelter where the animals rested, warm and quiet in the soft hay.

And there, in that gentle, holy place, baby Jesus was born. The Bible tells it so tenderly: "She wrapped him in bands of cloth, and laid him in a feeding trough, because there was no room for them in the inn" (Luke 2:7). Mary wrapped her tiny baby snug and warm, and laid Him in the manger where the animals were fed. The Son of God had come into the world, small and sweet, sleeping in the hay.

Outside the town, on the dark hillsides, some shepherds were watching their sheep beneath the stars. The night was still and quiet. Then, all at once, the sky lit up with glory, and an angel of the Lord stood before them! The shepherds were amazed, but the angel spoke softly to comfort them. "Don't be afraid," the angel said, "for behold, I bring you good news of great joy which will be to all the people. For there is born to you today, in David's city, a Savior, who is Christ the Lord" (Luke 2:10-11).

Then something even more beautiful happened. Suddenly the whole sky filled with angels, more than anyone could count, all praising God together. Their voices rang out across the hills: "Glory to God in the highest, on earth peace, good will toward men" (Luke 2:14). What a sound it must have been, the whole sky singing on the night the Savior was born!

When the angels had gone back to heaven, the shepherds looked at one another with wonder. "Let's go to Bethlehem," they said, "and see this wonderful thing that has happened." So they hurried through the night, down the quiet roads, until they found the place. And there He was, just as the angel had said: Mary, and Joseph, and the baby lying in the manger.

The shepherds were filled with joy. They had seen the newborn King. They knelt close and gazed at little Jesus, and then they went out telling everyone they met about the good news. Mary held it all in her heart, treasuring every gentle, glowing moment of that holy night.

This is what Christmas is all about. On that quiet, starry night, God gave the whole world the most wonderful gift of all: His own Son, baby Jesus. He came as a tiny baby in a manger to bring light into the world and peace to every heart. The little baby in the hay was the Savior the angels sang about, the One who loves us more than we can imagine.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down beneath your warm blanket, imagine that gentle stable long ago: the soft hay, the quiet animals, the bright star overhead, and baby Jesus sleeping peacefully in the manger. Imagine the sky full of singing angels and the joy in the shepherds' hearts. The same Jesus the angels praised is watching over you right now with so much love. Rest softly, little one. You are loved, and you are safe all through the night.
takeaway: On the first Christmas, God gave the world His Son, Jesus, who brings light, peace, and joy.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for the gift of baby Jesus on that holy night. Fill my heart with the peace and joy of Christmas, and help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "For there is born to you today, in David’s city, a Savior, who is Christ the Lord." — Luke 2:11
talkAboutIt: The angels brought the shepherds good news of great joy. What happy news would you want to run and tell someone you love?

title: Zacchaeus
subtitle: A story about being seen by Jesus
storyText:
In the busy town of Jericho there lived a man named Zacchaeus. He was a tax collector, in fact, he was the chief tax collector, and he had become very rich. But Zacchaeus was not happy. The people of the town did not like tax collectors, because they often took more money than they should. So Zacchaeus had plenty of coins, but very few friends, and his heart felt empty.

One day, wonderful news came to Jericho: Jesus was coming! Zacchaeus had heard about Jesus, the kind teacher who loved people that others passed by, and more than anything, he wanted to see Him. So he hurried out into the streets. But there was a problem. A huge crowd had gathered, and Zacchaeus was a short man. No matter how he stretched or stood on his toes, all he could see were the backs of taller people. He could not see Jesus at all.

But Zacchaeus did not give up. He ran ahead down the road, and there he saw a tall sycamore tree. Up he climbed, higher and higher, until he was perched on a branch with a perfect view. Now he could watch for Jesus over the heads of the whole crowd. Perhaps no one would even notice him up there.

Soon Jesus came walking down the road, with people pressing close all around Him. And then something amazing happened. When Jesus reached the tree, He stopped. He looked up, right into the branches, straight at Zacchaeus. Out of all the people in that crowd, Jesus saw him. And Jesus called him by his very own name: "Zacchaeus, hurry and come down, for today I must stay at your house" (Luke 19:5).

Zacchaeus could hardly believe it. Jesus knew his name! And Jesus wanted to come to his house, his house, where no one liked to visit! Zacchaeus scrambled down from the tree as fast as he could and welcomed Jesus joyfully. His lonely heart was filling up with happiness.

Some people in the crowd grumbled, wondering why Jesus would visit someone like Zacchaeus. But being noticed and loved by Jesus changed Zacchaeus from the inside out. Standing up tall, he made a wonderful promise. "Behold, Lord," he said, "half of my goods I give to the poor. If I have wrongfully exacted anything of anyone, I restore four times as much" (Luke 19:8). The man who once took too much now wanted to give, and to make right anything he had done wrong. His whole heart had changed.

Jesus was so glad. He said, "Today, salvation has come to this house." Then He told everyone the beautiful reason He had come at all: "For the Son of Man came to seek and to save that which was lost" (Luke 19:10). Jesus had not passed Zacchaeus by. He had come looking for him, all along.

What a gentle, wonderful truth this story holds. Jesus sees every single person, even the one up in the tree, even the one nobody else notices. No one is too small, too hidden, or too far away for His love. He knew Zacchaeus by name, and He knows your name too.

So tonight, little one, as you close your eyes and the house grows quiet, you can rest in this happy thought: Jesus sees you. He knows your name, He knows everything about you, and He loves you with a love that came looking just for you. You are never hidden from Him, and you are never alone. Sleep softly, dear one. The One who saw Zacchaeus in the tree is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: Jesus sees every person and comes looking for us in love; no one is too small for Him.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You for seeing me and knowing my name, just like You saw Zacchaeus. Thank You for loving me and coming to find me. Help me rest close to You tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "For the Son of Man came to seek and to save that which was lost." — Luke 19:10
talkAboutIt: Jesus saw Zacchaeus when everyone else looked past him. How does it feel to know that Jesus always sees you and knows your name?

title: Feeding the Five Thousand
subtitle: A story about God’s provision
storyText:
On a green hillside near the Sea of Galilee, a great crowd was following Jesus. They had walked a long way to hear Him teach and to watch Him heal the sick. There were so many people that no one could count them all—men, women, and little children, more than five thousand in all, sitting together on the grass.

The day grew long, and the sun began to slip toward evening. Jesus looked out over that huge crowd, and He knew the people were hungry. He turned to His friend Philip and asked where they could buy enough bread to feed everyone. Philip looked at the sea of faces and shook his head. Even a whole year of wages would not be enough to give each person just a little.

Then Andrew, another of Jesus' friends, brought someone forward. It was a little boy who had carried a small lunch with him. "There is a boy here who has five barley loaves and two fish, but what are these among so many?" (John 6:9). Five small loaves and two little fish, held in the hands of one child—it did not seem like much at all, not for a crowd as wide as a meadow.

But Jesus smiled. He told everyone to sit down on the soft grass. Then He took the little boy's loaves in His hands and lifted His eyes to heaven. "Jesus took the loaves; and having given thanks, he distributed to the disciples, and the disciples to those who were sitting down; likewise also of the fish as much as they desired" (John 6:11).

And here is the wonderful thing. The bread did not run out. The disciples carried piece after piece, basket after basket, walking up and down the hillside, and still there was more. Every hungry person ate until they were full and happy. The little fish kept coming too, as much as anyone wanted. The small gift in the boy's hands had become a feast for thousands.

When everyone had eaten their fill, Jesus did not want anything to be wasted. He said to His disciples, "Gather up the broken pieces which are left over, that nothing be lost" (John 6:12). So they went up and down, picking up every crumb. "So they gathered them up, and filled twelve baskets with broken pieces from the five barley loaves, which were left over by those who had eaten" (John 6:13).

Twelve whole baskets of leftovers! There was more food at the end than there had been at the beginning. The people looked at one another in wonder. They knew they had seen something only God could do.

It all began with one little boy who was willing to share what he had. His lunch seemed far too small. But when he placed it in Jesus' hands, Jesus made it more than enough—enough for everyone, with baskets to spare. Nothing we give to Jesus is ever too little. He takes our small gifts and our small selves, gives thanks for them, and does more than we could ever imagine.

So tonight, little one, if you ever feel small, or worried that you do not have enough, remember the boy on the hillside and his five loaves and two fish. The same Jesus who fed all those people is watching over you right now. Place your day in His hands, big parts and little parts, and rest. He always has more than enough love for you, and He will keep you safe all through the night.
takeaway: When we offer Jesus even the smallest gift, He makes it more than enough.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You for taking care of everyone on the hillside, and for taking care of me. Help me trust You with the things I have, big and small. Help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "They all ate, and were filled." — Matthew 14:20
talkAboutIt: A little boy shared his small lunch, and Jesus made it enough for thousands. What is something little you could share with someone tomorrow?

title: Jesus Loves the Children
subtitle: A story about welcome and love
storyText:
One day, while Jesus was teaching, mothers and fathers began carrying their little ones to Him. They wanted Jesus to lay His hands on the children and bless them. So they made their way through the crowd, holding tiny hands and carrying babies in their arms, hoping to come close to the gentle Teacher everyone was talking about.

But the disciples, Jesus' helpers, thought the children were too small to matter just then. They believed Jesus was far too busy and important for little ones. So they tried to send the families away. "Not now," they may have said. "Jesus has grown-up things to do."

When Jesus saw what was happening, He was not pleased at all. He stopped what He was doing and spoke up for the children. "Allow the little children to come to me! Don't forbid them, for God's Kingdom belongs to such as these" (Mark 10:14).

Imagine the children's faces lighting up as the great Teacher waved them over. The disciples stepped back, surprised, and the little ones came running and toddling straight to Jesus. Some of the youngest reached up their arms to be lifted. Some clung shyly to their mothers' hands until Jesus smiled, and then they came too. He was not too busy. He was never too busy. To Jesus, those children were some of the most welcome people of all.

In that time and place, many grown-ups thought children should be quiet and stay out of the way of important things. But Jesus saw it differently. He made sure everyone watching understood that little ones were precious to God—worth stopping for, worth blessing, worth holding. The crowd grew hushed as Jesus turned all His attention to the children.

Then Jesus said something surprising. He told the grown-ups that they had much to learn from the little ones. "Most certainly I tell you, whoever will not receive God's Kingdom like a little child, he will in no way enter into it" (Mark 10:15). A child trusts. A child reaches up to be held. A child believes with a happy, open heart. That, Jesus said, is exactly how everyone should come to God.

And then came the most tender moment of all. Jesus did not simply pat the children on the head and send them on their way. The Bible says, "He took them in his arms, and blessed them, laying his hands on them" (Mark 10:16). He gathered them up close, one by one, and held them. He looked into their eyes. He laid His kind hands gently on each small head and spoke God's blessing over them.

Picture it: the children safe in the strong, loving arms of Jesus, leaning their heads against Him, feeling completely welcome and completely loved. No one was too little. No one was sent away. Every single child belonged.

That is how Jesus feels about you, dear one. You are never too small for Him, never too quiet, never in the way. When you reach out to Him, He gathers you close, just as He gathered those children on that long-ago day. His arms are always open, and His love for you never runs out.

So tonight, little one, as you settle down to sleep, picture yourself among those happy children, walking right up to Jesus while He smiles and opens His arms. He welcomes you. He blesses you. He holds you close and tells you that you matter to God more than you could ever know. Rest now, safe and loved, for the same Jesus who held the children is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: You are never too small for Jesus—He welcomes you with open arms and loves you dearly.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You that You always welcome me and never send me away. Hold me close tonight the way You held the children, and help me feel how much You love me. Help me sleep peacefully. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Allow the little children to come to me! Don’t forbid them, for God’s Kingdom belongs to such as these." — Mark 10:14
talkAboutIt: Jesus took the children in His arms and blessed them. How does it feel to know that Jesus is never too busy for you?

title: The Prodigal Son
subtitle: A story about forgiveness and welcome
storyText:
One day, Jesus gathered a crowd around Him and told a story—a story to show just how much God loves us. It went like this.

There was once a father who had two sons. The younger son grew restless. He wanted to go far away and live however he pleased. So he asked his father for his share of the family's riches, and the kind father gave it to him. Soon the young man packed his bags and set off to a faraway country.

At first it seemed exciting. He spent his money on parties and foolish things, spending and spending until one day it was all gone. Then a great famine swept across the land, and there was little food anywhere. The young man, who had once had everything, now had nothing at all. To survive, he took the lowliest job he could find—feeding pigs in a muddy field. He was so hungry that even the pigs' food began to look good to him. No one gave him anything.

Sitting alone among the pigs, the young man finally began to think clearly. He remembered his father's house, where even the hired workers had plenty of food. "I will get up and go home," he decided. "I will tell my father how sorry I am." He practiced the words on the long road back: "Father, I have sinned. I am no longer worthy to be called your son." His feet were tired and his heart was heavy, for he did not know if his father would even want to see him.

But here is the most beautiful part. While the son was still a long way down the road, too far to see clearly, his father spotted him coming. He had been watching and waiting and hoping all that time. "While he was still far off, his father saw him, and was moved with compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him" (Luke 15:20).

The father did not wait at the door with his arms crossed. He ran—ran down the road to meet his boy, threw his arms around him, and kissed him again and again. The son began his sorry speech: "The son said to him, 'Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in your sight. I am no longer worthy to be called your son.'" (Luke 15:21). But the father was too full of joy to scold. He called for the finest robe to wrap around his son, a ring for his finger, and sandals for his tired feet. He ordered a great feast, and the whole house celebrated.

"For this, my son, was dead, and is alive again. He was lost, and is found." (Luke 15:24), the father said. His boy had come home, and that was all that mattered.

Jesus told this story so that everyone would know what God is like. God is the father on the road, watching, waiting, longing for His children to come home. When we wander away or make wrong choices, He does not turn His back. The moment we turn toward Him, He comes running with open arms, full of joy and forgiveness. There is nothing we could ever do that would make Him stop loving us.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down to sleep, remember the father who ran to meet his son. That is how God feels about you. You are loved, you are wanted, and you are always welcome in His arms. Rest in that good news, safe and forgiven, for the One who watches the road is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: No matter how far we wander, God runs to welcome us home with open arms.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for loving me like the father who ran to meet his son. When I make mistakes, help me remember that You always welcome me back. Help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "For this, my son, was dead, and is alive again. He was lost, and is found." — Luke 15:24
talkAboutIt: The father ran to hug his son and forgave him completely. When have you felt loved and forgiven, just the way God loves you?

title: Esther’s Courage
subtitle: A story about brave purpose
storyText:
Long ago, in the grand palace of a king named Ahasuerus, there lived a young woman named Esther. She had become the queen of all the land, wearing a royal crown and living in beautiful rooms. But Esther had a secret that only her cousin Mordecai knew: she was one of God's people, the Jews, who lived throughout the kingdom.

Now there was a proud and powerful man named Haman, who served the king. Haman did not like Mordecai, and his anger grew until he made a wicked plan—a plan to harm all of Esther's people, everywhere in the kingdom. When Mordecai heard of it, he was filled with sorrow. He sent an urgent message to Esther in the palace: she must go to the king and beg him to save their people.

But Esther was afraid. In those days, no one could go to the king without being invited—not even the queen. To walk in uninvited could cost a person their very life. Esther sent word back explaining the danger. Then Mordecai sent her words she would never forget: "Who knows if you haven't come to the kingdom for such a time as this?" (Esther 4:14). Perhaps, Mordecai was saying, God had made her queen for this very moment, to help her people when they needed her most.

Esther grew quiet and thought and prayed. She knew what she had to do. She sent this brave message back to Mordecai: "Go, gather together all the Jews who are present in Shushan, and fast for me… Then I will go in to the king, which is against the law; and if I perish, I perish." (Esther 4:16). For three days, Esther and all her people went without food and turned their hearts to God, asking Him for help.

Then, on the third day, Esther put on her royal robes and walked into the king's great hall. Her heart must have been pounding. She had no invitation. But when the king saw her standing there, he was not angry at all—he was glad. He held out his golden scepter to welcome her and asked what she wished for. God had gone before her and softened the king's heart.

Esther was wise and patient. She invited the king and Haman to a special dinner, and then to a second dinner the next day. At last, at just the right moment, she found the courage to speak. She told the king that someone was plotting to destroy her and all her people. "Who is it?" the king demanded. And brave Esther pointed to wicked Haman.

The king was furious at Haman's cruel plan. He stopped it at once. He gave Mordecai a place of honor, and he made a new law so that Esther's people could be safe and protected all across the land. The danger was over. God had used one brave queen to rescue an entire nation.

Esther had been so frightened. Yet she trusted God and did the right thing anyway—and that is what real courage is. Being brave does not mean you never feel afraid. It means you trust that God is with you, and you step forward to do what is good, even when your heart is trembling. God had placed Esther exactly where she needed to be, "for such a time as this."

So tonight, little one, if something ever feels too big or too scary, remember Queen Esther. God is with you, just as He was with her. He has a good plan for your life, and He can help you be brave. Rest now, safe and watched over, for the same God who gave Esther courage is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: Real courage is trusting God enough to do what is right, even when we feel afraid.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for being with brave Esther, and for being with me too. When something feels hard or scary, help me trust You and do what is right. Help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Who knows if you haven’t come to the kingdom for such a time as this?" — Esther 4:14
talkAboutIt: Esther was afraid but trusted God and was brave for her people. When is a time you can ask God to help you be brave?

title: Joshua and Jericho
subtitle: A story about trusting God’s way
storyText:
After their long journey, God's people, the Israelites, had finally come to the land God promised them. But standing in their way was the great city of Jericho. It had tall, thick walls all the way around it, and its gates were shut up tight. No one could go in, and no one could come out. To the people, those walls must have looked impossible to pass.

Their leader was a man named Joshua. He was strong and faithful, and God had told him not to be afraid. Then God gave Joshua a plan for Jericho—but it was not the kind of plan anyone expected. There would be no battering rams, no climbing ladders, no great battle at the gates. Instead, God said, "Behold, I have given Jericho into your hand, with its king and the mighty men of valor" (Joshua 6:2). The victory already belonged to God. The people only needed to trust Him and obey.

Here was God's surprising plan. Once each day, for six days, all the people were to march quietly around the city walls one time, while seven priests carried trumpets made of rams' horns and the holy ark of God went with them. Then, on the seventh day, they were to march around the city not once, but seven times. And after the seventh time around, when the priests gave a long blast on the trumpets, all the people were to shout with all their might.

It was an unusual plan. Marching in circles does not knock down walls! But Joshua trusted God completely. So the people began. Day one, they marched around Jericho one time and went back to camp. Day two, the same. Day three, four, five, six—around and around, quietly, faithfully, just as God had said. The people of Jericho watched from the high walls, wondering what it all could mean.

Then came the seventh day. Early in the morning, the people set out, and this time they marched around the city again and again—one, two, three, four, five, six, and on the seventh time around, the priests blew a long, loud blast on the trumpets. Joshua cried out, "Shout, for the LORD has given you the city!" And all the people shouted together with a mighty shout.

And then it happened. "The people shouted with a great shout, and the wall fell down flat, so that the people went up into the city, every man straight in front of him, and they took the city" (Joshua 6:20). The mighty walls of Jericho came tumbling down—not because of the people's strength, but because of God's power. What had looked impossible had crumbled at the sound of trust and obedience.

Joshua learned something wonderful that day. God's ways are not always the ways we would choose. Sometimes His plans seem surprising, even strange. But God is always wise, and His way is always best. When we trust Him and do what He asks, He can do what no person ever could.

God knew His people might feel small or afraid, so He had given Joshua a promise to carry in his heart: "Be strong and courageous. Don't be afraid… for the LORD your God is with you wherever you go." (Joshua 1:9). That promise was true at Jericho, and it is still true today.

So tonight, little one, if something ever feels as big and impossible as those tall city walls, remember Joshua and Jericho. You do not have to be big or strong on your own. God is with you wherever you go, and His plans are always good. Rest now, safe and unafraid, for the same mighty God who watched over Joshua is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: When we trust and obey God, He can do what looks impossible to us.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for being so strong and so wise. When something feels too big for me, help me trust You and remember that You are with me wherever I go. Help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Be strong and courageous. Don’t be afraid… for the LORD your God is with you wherever you go." — Joshua 1:9
talkAboutIt: God's plan for Jericho was surprising, but Joshua trusted Him anyway. What is something big you can ask God to help you with?

title: Elijah and the Whisper
subtitle: A story about God’s gentle voice
storyText:
The prophet Elijah had been through a very long and tiring time. He had done great things for God, but now he felt frightened and worn out and all alone. He had run far away into the wilderness, and at last he sat down under a little broom tree, too weary to go on. He was so tired that he fell fast asleep right there in the desert.

But God had not forgotten Elijah. While he slept, God sent an angel to care for him, ever so gently. The angel touched him softly and woke him, and there beside Elijah was fresh bread baking on warm stones and a jar of cool water to drink. Elijah ate and drank and lay down again. Then the angel came a second time, touched him again, and said, "Arise and eat, because the journey is too great for you." (1 Kings 19:7). God knew exactly what His tired servant needed—rest, and food, and tender care.

With his strength returned, Elijah traveled on until he came to a mountain of God. There he found a cave and went inside to rest, and God told him to come out and stand on the mountain, for the LORD was about to pass by.

Then something amazing began to happen. First came a great and powerful wind, so strong it tore at the mountains and broke the rocks into pieces. But the wind was not where God was speaking. After the wind came a rumbling earthquake that shook the ground—but God was not in the earthquake either. After the earthquake came a crackling fire—but God was not in the fire. The Bible tells it this way: "a great and strong wind tore the mountains… but the LORD was not in the wind. After the wind there was an earthquake; but the LORD was not in the earthquake" (1 Kings 19:11).

Elijah waited and listened. And then, after all the wind and shaking and fire had passed, something very different came. "After the fire, there was a still small voice." (1 Kings 19:12). It was a soft, gentle whisper—quiet as a breath, gentle as a feather. And there, in the hush, was God.

When Elijah heard that gentle whisper, he wrapped his face in his cloak and stepped to the mouth of the cave, for he knew that God was near. God spoke kindly to His tired prophet. He reminded Elijah that he was not alone after all, and He gave him good work still to do. Elijah did not need to be afraid anymore.

What a tender thing to learn. God is mighty enough to fill the wind and shake the earth and light the sky with fire. Yet when He drew close to comfort His weary friend, He came softly, in a quiet whisper. God does not only meet us in big, loud, exciting moments. Very often He comes to us in the still and gentle ones—when we are quiet, when we are resting, when the room grows hushed and peaceful.

So tonight, little one, as your room grows still and the lights go low, you can listen for God in the quiet too. He is close to you right now, gentle as a whisper, near as a breath. You are not alone, and you never will be. Rest now, calm and cared for, for the same God who whispered to Elijah and gave him rest is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: God is near in the quiet moments, gentle as a whisper, and we are never alone.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for caring for tired Elijah and for caring for me. As my room grows quiet tonight, help me feel how near You are. Help me sleep peacefully. Amen.
memoryVerse: "After the fire, there was a still small voice." — 1 Kings 19:12
talkAboutIt: God came to Elijah not in the loud wind or fire, but in a gentle whisper. What is a quiet moment when you feel close to God?

title: The Boy Samuel
subtitle: A story about listening for God
storyText:
The little town of Shiloh was quiet and dark. The lamp of God had not yet gone out in the house of the LORD, and inside, a boy named Samuel lay down to sleep near the holy place. Samuel was very young, but he already lived in God's house, helping a kind old priest named Eli. In those days, the Bible says, messages from the LORD were rare and precious. Hardly anyone heard God's voice at all.

But on this night, something wonderful was about to happen.

As Samuel was resting, he heard a voice call his name. "Samuel!" He sat up at once and ran to Eli, sure the old priest needed him. "Here I am," Samuel said, "for you called me." But Eli shook his head gently. "I didn't call you, my child. Go back and lie down." So Samuel padded back to his mat and lay down again in the soft darkness.

Then it happened again. "Samuel!" Once more the boy hurried to Eli. "Here I am, for you called me." And once more Eli said, "I didn't call, my son. Lie back down." A third time the voice came, and a third time Samuel ran to the old priest's side.

Now Eli understood. His tired eyes grew wide and tender, for he realized it was the LORD who was calling this little boy. So Eli told Samuel exactly what to do. "Go, lie down. It shall be, if he calls you, that you shall say, 'Speak, the LORD; for your servant hears.'" (1 Samuel 3:9) Samuel went back and lay down in his place, his heart beating softly, listening with all his might.

And the LORD came and stood there, and called as before: "Samuel! Samuel!" This time the boy did not run to Eli. This time he answered the One who had been calling all along. "Speak; for your servant hears." (1 Samuel 3:10)

What a beautiful thing—that the great God who made the stars and the sea would come close and call a small child by name, in the quiet of the night. Samuel did not have to be big or grown-up or important. He only had to listen and to say yes.

From that night on, Samuel grew up close to God. He listened, and he obeyed, and the LORD was always near him. The Bible tells us, "Samuel grew, and the LORD was with him, and let none of his words fall to the ground." (1 Samuel 3:19) Everything God gave Samuel to say came true, and all the people came to know that Samuel was a true friend of God.

It all began with a boy in the dark, learning to listen. God did not need a loud voice or a crowded room. He spoke softly, by name, to a child who was willing to hear Him.

You have a name too, little one—a name God knows and loves. He knows the color of your eyes and the thoughts inside your heart. And just like Samuel, you can have a quiet, listening heart that says, "Speak; I am listening." You can talk to God anytime, anywhere, and He always hears you.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down into your warm, soft bed, let your heart grow gentle and still like Samuel's. The same God who called Samuel by name in the dark knows your name too. He is near you right now, watching over you with love. You can close your eyes and whisper, "Speak, Lord, for I am listening"—and then rest, safe and peaceful, all through the night.
takeaway: God knows your name and speaks to a quiet, listening heart.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for knowing my name and loving me. Help me have a quiet heart that listens to You, just like Samuel did. Watch over me as I sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Speak; for your servant hears." — 1 Samuel 3:10
talkAboutIt: Samuel learned to say, "Speak, for I am listening." What is a quiet way you could listen for God this week?

title: Abraham and the Stars
subtitle: A story about trust
storyText:
Long, long ago, in a land of tents and starlit deserts, there lived a man named Abram.
