One day God would give him a new name—Abraham—but on this night he was simply Abram, an old man with a quiet ache in his heart. The LORD had promised him wonderful things, yet Abram and his wife Sarah had no children of their own, and Abram was growing old. He wondered, deep down, how God's promises could ever come true.

Then, in the stillness, the word of the LORD came to him. "Don't be afraid, Abram. I am your shield, your exceedingly great reward." (Genesis 15:1) What a gentle thing for God to say to a worried heart—do not be afraid. God Himself would be Abram's safe place and his greatest treasure.

But still Abram wondered about a child. So the LORD did something tender and surprising. He led Abram out of his tent, out under the wide-open night. Above them spread the whole shining sky, scattered with more stars than anyone could ever count—glittering and silver, near and far, all the way to the edges of the dark.

And the LORD said, "Look now toward the sky, and count the stars, if you are able to count them." (Genesis 15:5) Abram tipped back his head and looked up. He tried to count—one, two, three, ten, a hundred—but it was impossible. There were too many. They went on and on forever.

Then God made His promise plain: "So will your offspring be." Just as countless as those stars, Abram's family would one day be. Children and grandchildren, and their children after them, more than he could ever number, stretching far into the future like the lights of heaven.

Now, Abram could not see how this would happen. He had no child yet. He was old. From where he stood, the promise looked impossible. But Abram chose to trust the One who had spoken. The Bible says it simply and beautifully: "He believed in the LORD, who credited it to him for righteousness." (Genesis 15:6) Abram believed. He did not have to understand everything; he only had to trust the God who never breaks a promise.

And do you know what? God kept His word. After many years, Abram and Sarah held a baby boy of their very own, named Isaac—laughter and joy in their old age. And Isaac grew up and had children, and those children had children, on and on, until Abram's family truly did become as many as the stars. God's promise came true, exactly as He said it would, in His own perfect time.

Sometimes we wait a long while for good things. Sometimes a promise feels far away, like a star you can see but cannot touch. But the same God who counted out the stars and called each one to shine is faithful and kind. He sees you. He keeps every word He speaks. And He is never, ever in a hurry to forget you.

So tonight, little one, if you peeked out your window, you might see a star or two blinking in the dark. Each one is a tiny reminder of God's enormous promise to Abram—and of how trustworthy God truly is. You don't have to know how everything will work out. You can simply rest, like Abram did, believing that God is good and that He is watching over you.

So close your eyes now, dear one. The God who made the stars made you, and He is holding you safe. Sleep peacefully, and dream beneath the sky of the One who keeps His promises forever.
takeaway: Even when a promise feels impossible, God is faithful to keep His word.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for making the stars and for keeping every promise You make. Help me trust You like Abram did, even when I have to wait. Watch over me and help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "He believed in the LORD, who credited it to him for righteousness." — Genesis 15:6
talkAboutIt: God told Abram to count the stars he could never number. What is something you are trusting God for, even though you can't see how it will happen yet?

title: Ruth and Naomi
subtitle: A story about kindness
storyText:
In a faraway time, in the land of Moab, there lived a young woman named Ruth. Ruth had married into the family of a kind woman named Naomi. But sorrow came to that family, and Naomi's husband and both of her sons died. Now Naomi was alone, far from her homeland, with no one to care for her but her two daughters-in-law.

Naomi decided to go back to her own country, a place called Bethlehem. With tears in her eyes, she told the two young women to stay behind in Moab, where they had family and homes. One of them said goodbye. But Ruth would not leave. She wrapped her arms around Naomi and held on tight.

Then Ruth spoke words so loving that people have remembered them ever since. "Don't urge me to leave you, and to return from following you, for where you go, I will go; and where you stay, I will stay. Your people will be my people, and your God my God." (Ruth 1:16) Ruth was choosing to give up everything she knew so that Naomi would not be alone. She would go wherever Naomi went and stay wherever Naomi stayed, all the days of her life.

So the two women traveled together to Bethlehem. They were poor and had no food, so Ruth went out to work. In those days, when farmers gathered their barley, they left some behind in the fields for hungry people to pick up. Day after day, Ruth bent low in the hot sun, gathering grain so that she and Naomi would have something to eat. Her back grew tired and her hands grew sore, but she kept on working, because she loved Naomi.

A good and gentle man named Boaz owned one of those fields. When he saw Ruth working so hard, he asked about her, and he heard how kindly she had cared for Naomi. Boaz made sure Ruth was safe and gave her extra grain to carry home. He blessed her, saying, "May the LORD repay your work, and a full reward be given to you from the LORD, the God of Israel, under whose wings you have come to take refuge." (Ruth 2:12) Ruth had come to trust in God, and God was watching over her like a mother bird shelters her little ones beneath her wings.

In time, Boaz and Ruth were married, and what joy filled their home! God gave them a baby boy named Obed. Naomi, who had once felt so empty and sad, now held a grandchild in her arms. The women of Bethlehem rejoiced with her and said the baby would be a comfort to her old age, born to the daughter-in-law who loved her so. Naomi's emptiness had been filled all the way up with love.

And here is something wonderful: that little baby, Obed, grew up to be the grandfather of King David—and from David's family, long after, came Jesus Himself. Ruth's faithful kindness became part of the greatest story of all.

Kindness is a small and quiet thing, like a single seed dropped into the soil. But God can take our kindness and grow it into something beautiful and good, far bigger than we could ever imagine. When you stay beside someone who is sad, or share what you have, or work hard to help someone you love, God sees, and God smiles.

So tonight, little one, as you nestle down to sleep, think of Ruth and her loving heart. You are safe and warm, gathered under the wings of the God who never leaves us alone. Rest now, dear one, and dream of all the kindness you can plant tomorrow.
takeaway: Faithful kindness is a seed God grows into something beautiful.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for friends and family who love me. Help me be loyal and kind like Ruth, staying close to others when they are sad. Keep me safe under Your wings and help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Where you go, I will go; and where you stay, I will stay." — Ruth 1:16
talkAboutIt: Ruth promised Naomi, "Where you go, I will go." Who is someone you can stay close to and be kind to, especially when they feel lonely?

title: Moses and the Burning Bush
subtitle: A story about courage
storyText:
Far out in the wilderness, where the hills rolled brown and quiet, a man named Moses was watching over his sheep. Once Moses had lived in a palace, but now he lived a simple, hidden life, leading his flock across the wide and lonely land. On this day he had walked all the way to a mountain called Horeb, the mountain of God.

There, something caught his eye. A bush was on fire—flickering bright orange against the desert—but the strangest thing was that the bush did not burn up. The flames danced on and on, yet the leaves and branches stayed whole. Moses had never seen anything like it. "I will go closer," he said to himself, "and see this wonderful sight."

As he stepped near, a voice called to him from inside the bush. It was the voice of God. "Don't come close. Take off your sandals, for the place you are standing on is holy ground." (Exodus 3:5) Moses slipped off his sandals, and he hid his face, for he knew he was standing before the great God of heaven and earth.

Then God told Moses why He had come. "I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt, and have heard their cry because of their taskmasters, for I know their sorrows." (Exodus 3:7) God's people were far away in Egypt, working hard and hurting, and God had not forgotten them. He had heard every cry. He knew every tear. And now He was going to set them free—and He wanted Moses to help lead the way.

But Moses felt small and afraid. "Who am I," he asked, "that I should go? What if no one listens to me? I am not good with words." His heart trembled at such a big and frightening task.

God answered him with the most comforting promise of all. "Certainly I will be with you." (Exodus 3:12) Moses would not have to do this alone. The God who made the stars and the seas would walk right beside him every step of the way.

Moses asked, "But who shall I say has sent me? What is Your name?" And God gave an answer full of wonder: "I AM WHO I AM." (Exodus 3:14) God always was, and always is, and always will be. He never changes and never grows tired. That great and faithful God was the One sending Moses—and the One promising to go with him.

So Moses took a deep breath. He felt the warmth of God's nearness all around him, like a kind hand on his shoulder. And he said yes. Not because he had suddenly become brave all by himself, but because the God who is always with us had promised to be with him. Step by step, Moses went on to help free God's people, and great and wonderful things came to pass—all because he trusted that God would be near.

Sometimes we feel small too. Sometimes a new day brings something hard, and our hearts feel scared, just like Moses. But courage isn't about never being afraid. Courage is doing the next right thing while holding tightly to God's hand, knowing He has promised, "I will be with you."

So tonight, little one, as you settle into your soft, warm bed, you can let go of any worry about tomorrow. The same God who spoke to Moses from the burning bush is right here with you now. He knows your name, He hears your heart, and He will never leave you. Rest easy, brave one, and sleep peacefully in His care.
takeaway: Courage is doing the next right thing, knowing God has promised to be with you.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that You see me and know my name, just like You knew Moses. When something feels too big or scary, help me remember that You are with me. Give me a brave, peaceful heart and let me sleep gently tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Certainly I will be with you." — Exodus 3:12
talkAboutIt: God told Moses, "I will be with you." What is something hard that feels a little easier when you remember God goes with you?

title: The Walls of Water
subtitle: A story about trust
storyText:
After many long years in Egypt, God had set His people free at last. Moses led them out, a great crowd of mothers and fathers and children, all walking toward a new home God had promised them. But their journey brought them to the edge of a vast water called the Red Sea—wide and deep and stretching far beyond what they could see.

And then they heard a rumbling behind them. It was the king of Egypt and his army, racing after them in chariots, having changed their minds about letting God's people go. The sea lay ahead. The soldiers came from behind. The people felt trapped, and their hearts filled with fear. "What will we do?" they cried out.

But Moses spoke to them with calm, steady words. "Don't be afraid. Stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD, which he will work for you today." (Exodus 14:13) They did not need to fix this themselves. They only needed to trust and watch what God would do. Then Moses told them something every worried heart loves to hear: "The LORD will fight for you, and you shall be still." (Exodus 14:14)

That night, God placed a tall cloud between His people and the army behind them, so the soldiers could not reach them. And then God told Moses to stretch out his hand over the sea.

Moses lifted his staff over the water. All night long the LORD sent a strong east wind blowing across the sea. And the most amazing thing happened: the waters began to part. They drew back to the right and to the left, standing up tall like great glistening walls, and the ground between them turned dry and firm.

The Bible tells it just this way: "The children of Israel went into the middle of the sea on the dry ground, and the waters were a wall to them on their right hand, and on their left." (Exodus 14:22) Can you imagine it? A wide, dry path right through the middle of the sea, with shining walls of water standing on either side. The little children held their parents' hands and walked across, looking up in wonder at the water that did not fall. Step by step, all of God's people crossed safely to the other side.

When every last one of them stood safe on the far shore, the walls of water gently came back together, and the path closed behind them. God's people were free, and they were safe, and they were so full of joy that they sang and danced and thanked the LORD for rescuing them.

The people had thought they were trapped with no way out. But God made a way where there was no way at all. He did not ask them to be strong enough or brave enough on their own. He simply asked them to trust Him and stand still—and then He did the rescuing.

Sometimes things can feel that way for us, too. A problem can look as big as the sea, with no path through it. But the same God who opened the waters is still mighty and still kind, and nothing is too hard for Him. When you don't know what to do, you can do what God's people did: be still, and trust, and watch how He helps.

So tonight, little one, as you lie down in your cozy bed, you can let every worry go. The God who made a dry path through the deep sea is watching over you this very moment. You are safe. You are loved. Rest now, dear one, and sleep peacefully in the care of the One who always makes a way.
takeaway: When there seems to be no way, God can make one—we only need to trust and be still.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for being strong enough to help me with anything. When a problem feels as big as the sea, help me be still and trust You. Keep me safe and let me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "The LORD will fight for you, and you shall be still." — Exodus 14:14
talkAboutIt: God's people only had to be still and watch Him make a way. What is a worry you can give to God tonight instead of carrying it yourself?

title: Jacob's Ladder Dream
subtitle: A story about hope
storyText:
The sun was setting over the wilderness, painting the sky in soft pinks and purples, as a young man named Jacob walked alone. He had left his home far behind and was traveling toward the land of his relatives, with a long, long journey still ahead. He had no companions, no comfortable place to stay, and his heart felt heavy and unsure. Night was coming, and Jacob was all by himself.

When the light faded, Jacob stopped to rest in an open place. He had no soft pillow and no warm bed—only the ground beneath him and the wide sky above. So he found a smooth stone, laid his head upon it, and closed his tired eyes to sleep under the stars.

And there, in the quiet of that lonely night, Jacob had a dream more beautiful than any he had dreamed before. The Bible tells us: "He dreamed. Behold, a stairway set upon the earth, and its top reached to heaven. Behold, the angels of God ascending and descending on it." (Genesis 28:12) A shining stairway rose from the ground all the way up into heaven, and on it, God's angels were going up and coming down, up and down, glowing in the dark like living light. Earth and heaven were joined together, and Jacob could see it with his own eyes.

At the very top stood the LORD Himself. And God spoke to Jacob with the kindest, most comforting words a lonely traveler could ever hear. "Behold, I am with you, and will keep you, wherever you go, and will bring you again into this land. For I will not leave you, until I have done that which I have spoken of to you." (Genesis 28:15) I am with you. I will keep you. I will never leave you. Wherever Jacob wandered, near or far, God would be right there beside him, watching over him and bringing him safely home one day.

When Jacob woke up, the morning was new, and his heart was changed. He had thought he was all alone in an empty place—but he wasn't alone at all. "Surely the LORD is in this place," Jacob said in wonder, "and I didn't know it." (Genesis 28:16) God had been near him the whole time, even while he slept, even when he felt most lonely and afraid.

Jacob took the very stone he had rested his head upon and set it up tall, to remember that night forever. He called the place Bethel, which means "the house of God." And he carried God's promise with him for the rest of his journey, like a warm light tucked inside his heart. He was not afraid anymore, because now he knew the truth: God was with him, and God would keep His word.

Isn't it wonderful? Even in the dark, even far from home, even when Jacob felt most alone, God was right there. He never went away. And the very same God who watched over Jacob is watching over you, too. He is closer than the air you breathe, nearer than your own heartbeat, and He never sleeps and never looks away.

So tonight, little one, as you lay your head on your soft pillow and close your sleepy eyes, you can remember Jacob's dream and the stairway full of angels. You are never, ever alone. God is in this place—your room, your home, right beside your bed—watching over you with love. Rest now, dear one. Sleep gently and dream sweet dreams, safe in the care of the God who is always with you.
takeaway: Even when we feel alone, God is right beside us, keeping us wherever we go.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that I am never alone, because You are always with me. Watch over me tonight the way You watched over Jacob, and fill my heart with hope. Help me sleep peacefully and wake up glad. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Behold, I am with you, and will keep you, wherever you go." — Genesis 28:15
talkAboutIt: Jacob woke up and said, "Surely the LORD is in this place, and I didn't know it." Where is somewhere you can remember that God is close to you?

title: Gideon's Brave 300
subtitle: A story about courage
storyText:
Long ago, in the land of Israel, lived a man named Gideon. In those days an enemy people called the Midianites came every year and took the crops, and God's people were tired and afraid. But God had chosen Gideon to lead them, and God promised to be with him.

Gideon gathered an army by a spring of water, ready for the morning. There were thirty-two thousand men camped on the hillside. It seemed like a strong, brave army. But God saw something deeper than numbers. He wanted His people to know that the victory would come from Him, not from how big or strong they were.

So God spoke to Gideon. "The people who are with you are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hand, lest Israel brag against me, saying, 'My own hand has saved me.'" (Judges 7:2) Imagine that! God said there were too many soldiers. He did not want anyone to forget who was really saving them.

So Gideon told the men, "If anyone is afraid, you may go home." And twenty-two thousand soldiers went home. That left ten thousand. But God said, "Still too many." He had Gideon bring the men down to the water to drink. Most of them knelt down and put their faces to the stream. But three hundred men scooped the water up in their hands and lapped it while they watched, staying alert. God chose those three hundred.

Then God said the most wonderful, surprising thing. "By the three hundred men who lapped will I save you, and deliver the Midianites into your hand. Let all the other people go, each to his own place." (Judges 7:7) Just three hundred, against an enemy camp as countless as the sand. It seemed impossible. But Gideon trusted the God who had spoken.

That very night, God gave Gideon a gentle, clever plan. He divided the three hundred men into three companies. He gave each one a trumpet and a clay pitcher with a burning torch hidden inside. Quietly, quietly, in the dark, they tiptoed all around the edges of the sleeping enemy camp. Not a sound. Not a whisper. They waited for Gideon's signal.

Then, all at once, the three hundred blew their trumpets and smashed their pitchers, and the torches blazed bright in the night. "The three companies blew the trumpets, broke the pitchers, and held the torches in their left hands, and the trumpets in their right hands with which to blow; and they shouted, 'The sword of the LORD and of Gideon!'" (Judges 7:20) The whole valley rang with the sound.

The enemy soldiers woke up startled in the dark, with trumpets blaring and lights flashing all around them. They were so confused and frightened that they began to run. "They each stood in his place around the camp; and all the army ran; and they shouted, and put them to flight." (Judges 7:21) God's people were rescued, and not by the size of their army at all. Just three hundred men, some trumpets, some torches, and a God who keeps His promises.

You see, little one, Gideon's three hundred remind us of a beautiful truth. You do not have to be the biggest or the strongest or have everything figured out. When God is with you, even something small and trembling can be brave. He loves to do wonderful things through people who simply trust Him.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down, remember Gideon and his three hundred. The same God who turned a tiny band of soldiers into something mighty is watching over you. You can rest, because you do not have to be strong all by yourself. God is strong, and God is with you, all through the night.
takeaway: We don't have to be big or strong to be brave, because God gives the victory when we trust Him.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that I don't have to be the strongest, because You are strong for me. Help me be brave and trust You like Gideon did. Help me rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "By the three hundred men who lapped will I save you, and deliver the Midianites into your hand." — Judges 7:7
talkAboutIt: God saved His people with just three hundred and some trumpets. When is a time you felt small but God helped you be brave?

title: Elijah and the Ravens
subtitle: A story about trust
storyText:
Long ago, in the land of Israel, lived a prophet named Elijah. A prophet is someone who listens to God and speaks God's words to others. In those days the king of Israel, a man named Ahab, had turned away from God, and the people had begun to forget the One who made them. But Elijah believed in God with all his heart.

God sent Elijah with a message for the king: because the people had wandered so far from God, there would be no rain for a long time. No rain meant the streams would shrink and the crops would not grow. It was a hard message to carry. But after Elijah spoke it, God did not leave him alone to worry. God had a gentle plan to take care of him.

"Go from here," God told Elijah, "and hide yourself by the brook Cherith, that is before the Jordan." So Elijah walked out to a quiet little brook in the wilderness, far from the city, where cool water still trickled over the stones. And there God made him a promise that must have sounded almost too wonderful to believe. "You shall drink from the brook. I have commanded the ravens to feed you there." (1 Kings 17:4)

Now think about that for a moment, little one. Out in the wilderness there was no store, no baker, no kitchen. There was no one at all to bring Elijah his supper. How would he eat? But God said the ravens would bring him food. Ravens are big black birds, and birds do not usually share their food with people. Yet God is the One who made every bird, and the ravens would do exactly what God told them to do.

And so they did. Morning after morning, the ravens came flying through the sky. Evening after evening, they came again, carrying bread and meat in their beaks and laying it down for Elijah to eat. The Bible tells it simply and beautifully: "The ravens brought him bread and meat in the morning, and bread and meat in the evening; and he drank from the brook." (1 Kings 17:6)

Every single day, food arrived. Every single day, the brook gave him cool water to drink. Elijah never had to lie awake wondering, "Will there be anything to eat tomorrow?" He trusted that God would care for him, and God always did, with food carried on the wings of birds.

Isn't it lovely to think that God can use anything at all to take care of His children? He used a quiet brook and a flock of ravens. He knew exactly what Elijah needed, and He knew right where Elijah was. God was watching over that little brook in the wilderness the whole time.

And here is the heart of it, little one. The same God who fed Elijah by the brook is your God too. He sees you. He knows your name and everything you need. You do not have to carry your worries all by yourself, because the One who feeds the birds of the sky loves you far more than the birds.

So tonight, little one, as you close your eyes and snuggle deep into your warm bed, you can let every worry float away. God took care of Elijah morning and evening, and God is taking care of you. You are seen, you are loved, and you are safe in His keeping all through the night. Rest now, little one, for the God who never forgets is watching over you.
takeaway: God sees exactly what we need and cares for us in wonderful ways, so we can trust Him completely.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that You always know what I need, just like You knew what Elijah needed. Help me trust You and not worry. Help me rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "I have commanded the ravens to feed you there." — 1 Kings 17:4
talkAboutIt: God sent ravens to bring Elijah food every morning and evening. What is something God gives you each day that you can thank Him for?

title: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego
subtitle: A story about courage
storyText:
Long ago, in the faraway land of Babylon, lived three friends with three special names: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. They had been brought far from their home in Israel, but wherever they went, they loved God and stayed true to Him with all their hearts.

The king of Babylon was named Nebuchadnezzar, and he was a very powerful king. One day he built an enormous golden statue, tall and shining, and he made a grand announcement. When the music played, everyone in the whole kingdom was to bow down low and worship the golden statue. Anyone who would not bow down would be thrown into a blazing furnace.

The music played. All across the land, people knelt and bowed. But three friends stood quietly tall. Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego would not bow down, because they worshiped only the one true God. Some people noticed, and they hurried to tell the king.

The king was furious, but he gave the three friends one more chance to bow. They stood close together, calm and unafraid, and gave a brave and beautiful answer. "If it happens, our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace; and he will deliver us out of your hand, O king. But if not, let it be known to you, O king, that we will not serve your gods or worship the golden image which you have set up." (Daniel 3:17-18) Their God could rescue them, they said. But even if He chose not to, they would never, ever bow to anyone but Him.

The king was angrier than ever. He ordered the furnace heated up, and the strong soldiers led the three friends to the fire. But God had not left His children alone, not for a single moment.

The king looked into the furnace, and his eyes went wide with wonder. He had thrown in three men, but now he counted again. "Look, I see four men loose, walking in the middle of the fire, and they are unharmed. The appearance of the fourth is like a son of the gods." (Daniel 3:25) Four! There in the flames, the three friends were walking about, safe and calm, and beside them shone a fourth, sent by God Himself to be with them. The fire could not hurt them at all.

The king called them to come out. Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego stepped out of the furnace, and everyone gathered close to see. Not a hair on their heads was singed. Their clothes were not burned. There was not even the smell of smoke on them. God had kept them perfectly safe.

Then the proud king bowed his own heart low and praised the God he had once defied. "Blessed be the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, who has sent his angel, and delivered his servants who trusted in him." (Daniel 3:28) The whole kingdom saw how great and faithful God is.

Here is the gentle truth at the heart of it, little one. The three friends were not brave because they were strong. They were brave because they trusted God, and God was right there with them in the middle of the hard thing. He never left their side.

So tonight, little one, as you settle into your soft, safe bed, remember Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. Whenever something feels scary or hard, you do not have to face it all alone. The same God who walked with three friends in the fire is right beside you now, holding you close. You are safe. You are loved. Rest gently, little one, for God is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: When we stay true to God, He is right beside us, even in the hardest and scariest moments.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that You are always with me, even when things feel scary. Help me be brave and stay close to You like Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. Help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace." — Daniel 3:17
talkAboutIt: The three friends trusted that God was with them even in the fire. Who or what helps you feel brave when something is scary?

title: Hannah's Prayer
subtitle: A story about prayer
storyText:
Long ago, in the land of Israel, lived a kind woman named Hannah. Hannah loved God, and Hannah had a quiet ache in her heart. More than anything in the world, she longed to have a baby of her own. But year after year went by, and no baby came. Her heart grew heavy and sad, and sometimes she could not even eat.

Every year, Hannah and her family traveled to the house of the LORD in a place called Shiloh, to worship and to pray. One day there, Hannah's heart was so full that she rose and went to the LORD all by herself. She did not ask another person to fix her sadness. She brought it straight to God.

Hannah wept, and she prayed, and she made a promise to God from the very bottom of her heart. "She vowed a vow, and said, 'the LORD of Armies, if you will indeed look at the affliction of your servant, and remember me, and not forget your servant, but will give to your servant a boy, then I will give him to the LORD all the days of his life.'" (1 Samuel 1:11) If God gave her a little boy, she would give him right back to God, to love and serve Him all his life.

Hannah prayed silently, deep inside her heart. Her lips were moving, but no sound came out. Now, an old priest named Eli was sitting nearby, and when he saw her lips moving without a sound, he did not understand at first. But Hannah gently explained. "No, my lord, I am a woman of a sorrowful spirit," she said. "I have not been drinking wine or strong drink, but I poured out my soul before the LORD." (1 Samuel 1:15) She had simply poured out all her feelings to God, like emptying a full cup into His loving hands.

Eli's heart softened, and he gave her a gentle blessing. "Go in peace; and may the God of Israel grant your petition that you have asked of him." (1 Samuel 1:17) And something wonderful happened to Hannah right there. Her sadness lifted. Her face was no longer heavy, because she had given her whole heart to God, and she trusted Him to hear her.

And God did hear her. After a while, God gave Hannah the very thing she had asked for: a baby boy. She named him Samuel, which means "asked of God," because she had asked God for him. Hannah was filled with joy and thankfulness. She had not forgotten her promise, and she raised Samuel to love and serve the LORD. Samuel grew up to become one of the greatest prophets in all of Israel, a man who listened closely to God's voice his whole life long.

Here is the heart of it, little one. Hannah teaches us that we can tell God exactly how we feel. When our hearts are heavy or happy, hopeful or sad, we can pour all of it out to Him, even quietly, even without making a sound. God hears every single word, and even the prayers no one else can hear at all. He listens with great love, and He answers in His own perfect way and time.

So tonight, little one, as you nestle down into your cozy bed, remember Hannah and her quiet, trusting prayer. You can talk to God about anything at all, right where you are. He is always listening, and He loves you very much. Whisper your prayers to Him, then close your eyes and rest, for God hears you, and He is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: We can pour out our whole heart to God in prayer, and He always hears us with great love.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that You hear every prayer, even the quiet ones in my heart. Help me talk to You about everything, just like Hannah did. Help me rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "I poured out my soul before the LORD." — 1 Samuel 1:15
talkAboutIt: Hannah poured out her whole heart to God in prayer. What is something you would like to quietly talk to God about tonight?

title: The Garden of Eden
subtitle: A story about love
storyText:
In the very beginning, God made the whole world: the sky and the seas, the sun and the moon and the bright shining stars. And then God made something especially beautiful, a garden, just for the people He loved. "the LORD God planted a garden eastward, in Eden, and there he put the man whom he had formed." (Genesis 2:8)

Oh, what a garden it was! Trees grew tall and heavy with sweet, delicious fruit. Flowers bloomed in every color you could imagine. A gentle river wound its way through the green, watering everything, and the air was soft and warm. God made the first man, Adam, and the first woman, Eve, and He gave this whole wonderful garden to them as a home.

God gave Adam a gentle, happy job. "the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden to cultivate and keep it." (Genesis 2:15) Adam got to care for the garden, to tend its plants and watch it grow. He even gave names to all the animals, one by one. There was no sickness in Eden, no fear, no tears. Only goodness, and beauty, and love.

And here is the most wonderful part of all. God Himself loved to come and be with Adam and Eve. In the cool of the evening, when the day grew soft and golden, God would walk in the garden, and He and the people He made would be together. Imagine that, little one: the great God who made the stars wanted to walk with His children, like the closest of friends. That is how very much He loved them.

Now, God had given Adam and Eve just one gentle rule, to help them and keep them safe. There was one tree in the middle of the garden whose fruit they were not to eat. But one day they made a sad choice, and they ate from that tree anyway. And as soon as they did, their hearts felt heavy and afraid for the very first time. When they heard God walking in the garden in the cool of the day, they were so afraid that they hid themselves away among the trees.

But listen, little one, to what God did then, for it shows us His tender heart. God did not give up on them. He did not stop loving them. "the LORD God called to the man, and said to him, 'Where are you?'" (Genesis 3:9) Now, God knew exactly where Adam was hiding. He always knows where we are. But like a loving parent calling softly for a child, God came looking for them. He wanted them back close to Him.

God was sad about the wrong choice, but He cared for Adam and Eve even still. He made them warm clothes with His own hands, and He made a promise that one day He would send Someone to make everything right again, and to bring His children close to Him forever. From the very beginning, God's plan was full of love.

That is the heart of this story, little one. Even when people wander away, God comes looking for them. He calls our names gently. He never stops loving us, not for a single moment. The same God who walked in the garden long ago still wants to be close to you today.

So tonight, little one, as you close your eyes in your warm and cozy bed, remember the garden God made out of love. Remember how He walked with His children and called them by name. That same loving God knows right where you are this very minute, and He is so glad you are His. You are loved, you are safe, and He is watching over you all through the night.
takeaway: God made us out of love, and even when we wander, He comes looking for us and calls us close.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for making such a beautiful world and for loving me so much. Even when I make mistakes, thank You for never stopping looking for me. Help me rest peacefully in Your love tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "We love him, because he first loved us." — 1 John 4:19
talkAboutIt: God walked in the garden and called, "Where are you?" because He wanted to be close. What would you like to tell God if you walked together tonight?

title: Joseph Forgives His Brothers
subtitle: A story about kindness
storyText:
Long ago lived a young man named Joseph, one of twelve brothers. When Joseph was a boy, his older brothers had grown jealous of him, and they did a very unkind thing: they sold him away to travelers, who carried him far from home to the land of Egypt. For many long years, Joseph was far from his family. Some of those years were hard and lonely.

But through it all, Joseph never stopped trusting God, and God was with him every step of the way. Joseph worked honestly and treated everyone with kindness. And in time, God lifted Joseph up to a place of great honor. He became a ruler in Egypt, second only to the king himself, in charge of storing up food. This was important, because a great famine was coming, a time when no food would grow, not just in Egypt but in many lands all around.

When the famine came, people traveled from far away to buy food in Egypt. And one day, who should arrive but Joseph's own brothers, hungry and looking for grain to feed their family. They bowed low before the great ruler, never dreaming that he was the little brother they had sold so long ago. But Joseph knew them at once. His heart leaped inside him.

Joseph could have stayed angry. He could have sent them away. But Joseph's heart was full of God's love and forgiveness. At last he could hold it in no longer. He sent everyone else out of the room, and he wept, and he said the words his brothers never expected to hear. "Come near to me, please," he told them. And when they came close, he said gently, "I am Joseph, your brother, whom you sold into Egypt." (Genesis 45:4)

His brothers were frightened and ashamed. But Joseph spoke to them with the kindest, most comforting words. "Now don't be grieved, nor angry with yourselves, that you sold me here, for God sent me before you to preserve life." (Genesis 45:5) Joseph saw something beautiful: that even through the hard years, God had been working to bring good, so that one day Joseph could save many people, including his own family, from hunger.

Then Joseph threw his arms around his brothers and hugged them tight, and they wept together with joy. The hurt of so many years melted away in the warmth of his forgiveness. Joseph invited his whole family, his father and all his brothers, to come and live near him in Egypt, where there would be plenty of food. He took loving care of them all.

Years later, Joseph would say it once more, in words as gentle as a soft blanket: "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring to pass, as it is today, to save many people alive." (Genesis 50:20) Joseph had chosen, again and again, to forgive instead of to stay angry. And in his forgiving heart, his whole family found peace.

Here is the heart of it, little one. Forgiveness is one of the most beautiful gifts we can ever give. When someone hurts us, staying angry only makes our hearts feel heavy and tight. But choosing to forgive, the way Joseph did, makes our hearts feel light and free and full of love. And we can forgive because God first forgave us, and He helps us when forgiving feels hard.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down warm in your bed, think of Joseph and his happy, forgiving heart. If anything has made your heart feel heavy today, you can give it gently to God before you sleep. He will help you let it go. You are loved, you are forgiven, and God is watching over you with tender care all through the night. Rest now, little one.
takeaway: Choosing to forgive, the way Joseph did, fills our hearts with peace and lets God's love shine through us.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for always forgiving me and loving me. Help me have a kind, forgiving heart like Joseph, even when someone hurts me. Help me rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good." — Genesis 50:20
talkAboutIt: Joseph forgave his brothers and his heart felt light and free. Is there anything you'd like to forgive and give to God before you sleep tonight?

title: Miriam's Song
subtitle: A story about hope
storyText:
When the people of Israel came up out of the Red Sea, they could hardly believe their eyes. For years and years they had been slaves in the land of Egypt, working hard from morning to night. But God had set them free. He had opened a dry path right through the middle of the sea, with walls of water standing tall on either side, and led His people safely to the other shore.

Now they stood on the far bank, safe and sound, looking back at the wide water. They were free at last! Their hearts were so full that singing was the only thing big enough to hold all their thankfulness. So Moses and the people lifted up their voices to God and sang, "I will sing to the LORD, for he has triumphed gloriously" (Exodus 15:1).

Among them was a woman named Miriam. She was a prophetess, the sister of Aaron and of Moses himself. When Miriam was just a girl, she had watched over baby Moses among the reeds by the river, and now here she was, watching God keep every promise He had made. Her heart could not stay still.

Miriam picked up a tambourine—a little drum ringed with bells that jingle when you shake it. She lifted it high and began to play and dance. And all the women came out after her, with tambourines of their own and with dancing, until the whole shore rang with happy music.

The Bible tells us, "Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took a tambourine in her hand; and all the women went out after her with tambourines and with dances" (Exodus 15:20). Then Miriam sang back to them the song of God's great rescue: "Sing to the LORD, for he has triumphed gloriously" (Exodus 15:21).

Round and round they danced beneath the wide sky. The little children clapped their hands and skipped along. Mothers swung their babies and laughed. The desert that had felt so frightening only the day before was now filled with the sound of joy.

Miriam's song was not only a happy song. It was a remembering song. With every beat of the tambourine, the people remembered how strong God was, how He had seen them when they were sad, and how He had carried them to safety. Their song said, "Look what God has done!"

And it was a hoping song, too. God had brought them this far, and that meant they could trust Him for all the days still ahead. The same God who opened the sea would walk with them every step of the journey to come. When you remember the good things God has already done, your heart fills up with hope for tomorrow.

You can sing to God too, little one. When you are happy, when you are thankful, when something wonderful happens—you can lift up a song just like Miriam did. God loves to hear the songs of His children, even the quiet ones you sing only in your heart.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down under your blanket, you can hum a soft and happy tune inside. Think of Miriam dancing by the sea, and of how good God has been to you today. He is watching over you with great love, and He will keep you safe all through the night. Sweet dreams, dear one.
takeaway: When we remember what God has done, our hearts overflow with joy and hope.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for all the wonderful things You have done for me. Help my heart sing with joy and hope, even when I am quiet. Watch over me and help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: “Sing to the LORD, for he has triumphed gloriously.” — Exodus 15:21
talkAboutIt: Miriam sang and danced because she remembered how God had saved her people. What good thing did God do for you today that you could thank Him for?

title: Nehemiah Builds the Wall
subtitle: A story about courage
storyText:
Far away from his home in Jerusalem, a man named Nehemiah served as cupbearer to the great king of Persia. It was an important job in the king's palace. But one day Nehemiah's heart grew heavy, for travelers brought him sad news: the city of Jerusalem still lay in ruins. Its strong walls had been broken down long ago, and its gates had been burned with fire. Without walls, the people who lived there felt unsafe and ashamed.

Nehemiah loved Jerusalem, and the news made him weep. But he did not stay stuck in his sadness. First, before anything else, he prayed. For days he prayed to the God of heaven, asking Him to help. Then, when the king noticed his sad face and asked what was wrong, Nehemiah breathed another quick prayer and bravely asked the king to send him to rebuild the city. And God moved the king's heart to say yes.

So Nehemiah traveled the long road to Jerusalem. When he arrived, he did not rush. By night he quietly rode around the broken walls, looking carefully at every fallen stone and burned gate, so he would know exactly what needed to be done. Only then did he gather the people together.

"You see the bad situation that we are in," he told them, "how Jerusalem lies waste, and its gates are burned with fire. Come, let us build up the wall of Jerusalem, that we won't be disgraced" (Nehemiah 2:17). Then he told them how the good hand of his God had been upon him. And the people answered, "Let's rise up and build." So they strengthened their hands for the good work.

Everyone joined in. Some families repaired the gate nearest their own homes. Some carried heavy stones, some mixed mortar, some lifted the walls higher and higher. Priests and rulers, sons and daughters—all worked side by side. The Bible says, "So we built the wall... for the people had a mind to work" (Nehemiah 4:6). When God's people work together, even an enormous job begins to feel possible.

But not everyone was glad. Some unfriendly men named Sanballat and Tobiah did not want Jerusalem to be strong again. They laughed at the builders. They made threats and tried to scare the workers into stopping. They even tried to trick Nehemiah into leaving the wall.

Nehemiah did not give in to the fear. He prayed again, and he was wise. He set guards to keep watch, and the workers kept building with a tool in one hand, ready to protect their families with the other. To the men who tried to stop them, Nehemiah said with quiet confidence, "The God of heaven will prosper us. Therefore we, his servants, will arise and build" (Nehemiah 2:20). And so the work went on, day after day, even when it was hard.

At last the great day came. After all the lifting and building and praying, "the wall was finished in the twenty-fifth day of Elul, in fifty-two days" (Nehemiah 6:15). Only fifty-two days! When the people who had mocked them heard, they understood that this work had been done with the help of God. The city stood safe and whole again, and Jerusalem was filled with thanksgiving.

Nehemiah's story shows us something worth remembering: when a problem looks far too big, we can do what Nehemiah did. We can pray first, then begin the work, keep going even when others try to discourage us, and trust God to give us strength. Big, impossible-looking walls can be rebuilt, one stone at a time.

So tonight, little one, as you rest your head and let the day go quiet, you can remember Nehemiah. Whatever feels too big for you, you never carry it alone. You can pray about it, just as he did, and then sleep in peace—for the God of heaven who helped Nehemiah is watching over you, and He is strong enough for anything. Rest well, dear one.
takeaway: When we pray first and trust God, He gives us courage to do hard things.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God of heaven, thank You for being strong when I am small. Help me to pray first, work hard, and stay brave when things are difficult. Watch over me and give me peaceful rest tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: “The God of heaven will prosper us.” — Nehemiah 2:20
talkAboutIt: Nehemiah prayed before he ever picked up a stone. What is something a little too big for you that you could pray about, and then work on bit by bit?

title: David the Shepherd Boy
subtitle: A story about peace
storyText:
Long ago, before he was ever a king, David was a shepherd boy. Every day he led his family's sheep out across the green hills to find good grass and cool water. He carried a stick called a staff to guide them, and a sling to keep them safe. It was a quiet, gentle job, and David grew to love it.

David knew his sheep, and his sheep knew him. When they wandered too far, he gently called them back, and they came running, for they trusted his kind voice. When they were thirsty, he found them a quiet stream where the water was calm and easy to drink. When the sun grew hot, he led them to a shady, restful place to lie down. And at night, when wild animals crept near, brave young David stood watch and kept his little flock safe. The sheep could rest without a single worry, because their shepherd was always near.

As David sat on the hillside under the wide and starry sky, he thought about something wonderful. The way he loved and cared for his sheep was the very way God loved and cared for him. He was God's own little lamb. And so David made up a song, one of the most beautiful songs ever written, called the Twenty-third Psalm.

"The LORD is my shepherd," David sang, "I shall lack nothing" (Psalm 23:1). That means: God is taking care of me, so I have everything I truly need.

Then David sang of how gently his Shepherd leads: "He makes me lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside still waters. He restores my soul" (Psalm 23:2-3). Can you picture it? Soft green grass to rest upon, calm quiet water to drink, and a tired heart made peaceful and new again. That is just how God cares for those He loves.

David knew that even in dark and scary places, he never had to be afraid. "Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death," he sang, "I will fear no evil, for you are with me. Your rod and your staff, they comfort me" (Psalm 23:4). A good shepherd carries his staff to guide his sheep and his rod to protect them. David trusted that God was guarding him in just the same way—always close, always watching, even when the path grew shadowy.

And David's song ended with a happy promise: that God's goodness and loving kindness would follow him all the days of his life, and that he would dwell in the LORD's house forever. What a comfort that is! God's love does not run out. It follows us all day long, and it stays right beside us all night, too.

You are God's precious little lamb, dear one, just as David was. God knows your name. He knows exactly what you need. He leads you to good and gentle places, and He is watching over you this very moment, while you are tucked safely into bed. You never have to be afraid, because your Shepherd is always near.

So tonight, little one, as you close your eyes, let your heart be calm and still, like a quiet stream in the moonlight. The Good Shepherd who watched over David is watching over you. He will keep you safe and peaceful all through the night. Rest now, sweet lamb. Sweet dreams.
takeaway: God is our Good Shepherd who loves us, leads us, and keeps us safe.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, be my Good Shepherd tonight. Thank You for staying close to me so I never have to be afraid. Lead me to peaceful rest and watch over me while I sleep. Amen.
memoryVerse: “The LORD is my shepherd: I shall lack nothing.” — Psalm 23:1
talkAboutIt: David said the LORD is his shepherd who leads him to quiet, restful places. What is a calm place or moment that makes you feel safe and close to God?

title: Solomon Asks for Wisdom
subtitle: A story about prayer
storyText:
Solomon was a young man when he became king of Israel. His father, King David, had ruled for many years, and now the great task had passed to Solomon. All the people of the land would look to him. He would have to lead them, settle their arguments, and decide what was right and what was wrong. It was an enormous responsibility for someone so young, and Solomon knew it.

One night, Solomon traveled to a place called Gibeon to worship God. And while he slept, the LORD came to him in a dream. God said to him something amazing: "Ask for what I should give you" (1 Kings 3:5). Imagine that! God offered to give Solomon whatever he asked. He could have asked for anything in all the world.

Solomon could have asked to be rich, with gold and treasure beyond counting. He could have asked to live a very long life, or to win every battle against his enemies. Those are the kinds of things many people would have wished for. But Solomon thought carefully about what he truly needed.

He knew he was young, and the job before him was too big for him to do on his own. So this is what he asked: "Give your servant therefore an understanding heart to judge your people, that I may discern between good and evil; for who is able to judge this great people of yours?" (1 Kings 3:9). Solomon did not ask for things for himself. He asked for wisdom—so that he could take good care of the people God had given him.

And God was delighted with Solomon's prayer. The LORD said, "Because you have asked this thing, and have not asked for yourself long life, nor have you asked for riches for yourself, nor have you asked for the life of your enemies, but have asked for yourself understanding to discern justice" (1 Kings 3:11)—because of that, God would give him exactly what he asked for, and far more besides.

"Behold," God said, "I have given you a wise and understanding heart; so that there has been no one like you before you, and after you none will arise like you" (1 Kings 3:12). And because Solomon had asked so unselfishly, God promised to give him riches and honor as well, things he had not even asked for.

When Solomon woke from his dream, his heart was full. He had asked for the right thing, and God had heard him. From that day on, Solomon became known as the wisest person in all the land. When people had hard, tangled problems they could not solve, they came from far and near to King Solomon, and his wise and gentle answers helped them find their way.

There is something beautiful to learn from Solomon's prayer. What we ask God for shows what we truly treasure in our hearts. Solomon did not reach first for money or fame. He reached for wisdom and an understanding heart—the kind of heart that knows how to love others, tell right from wrong, and make good and fair choices. And God loves to give those good gifts to anyone who asks.

You can pray the way Solomon prayed. When you have a hard choice to make, or you are not sure what is right, you can ask God to give you an understanding heart. He listens to every prayer, even the small ones whispered at bedtime, and He delights to help you grow wise and kind.

So tonight, little one, as you settle down to sleep, you can quiet your heart and think about what matters most. Ask God to help you love well and choose well tomorrow. He hears you, He cares for you, and He is watching over you all through the night. Rest peacefully now, dear one.
takeaway: When we ask God for what truly matters, like a wise and understanding heart, He is glad to help us.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, like Solomon, I ask You for an understanding heart. Help me to love others well and to choose what is right and kind. Thank You for always listening, and please give me peaceful rest tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: “Give your servant therefore an understanding heart to judge your people, that I may discern between good and evil.” — 1 Kings 3:9
talkAboutIt: Solomon could have asked God for anything, and he asked for wisdom to help others. If God said to you, 'Ask for whatever you want,' what would you ask for, and why?

title: Jesus Walks on Water
subtitle: A story about trust
storyText:
After a long day of teaching and caring for the crowds, Jesus told His friends, the disciples, to get into their boat and sail across the lake without Him. Then He went up onto a mountain by Himself to pray. As the sun went down and the stars came out, Jesus stayed there alone, talking quietly with His Father in heaven.

Out on the water, the disciples were rowing hard. But the night grew rough. The wind began to blow against them, stronger and stronger, and the waves rose up and rocked the little boat. They rowed and rowed, but the wind kept pushing them back. By the deepest part of the night, they were tired and worried, far from shore.

Then, in the dark, they saw something they could hardly believe. Someone was coming toward them—walking on top of the water! The disciples were frightened. "It's a ghost!" they cried. But the figure on the waves called out to them in a voice they knew and loved: "Cheer up! It is I! Don't be afraid" (Matthew 14:27). It was Jesus, walking across the sea as easily as if it were dry ground.

Peter, one of the disciples, felt his fear melt into wonder. "Lord," he called, "if it is You, tell me to come to You on the water." And Jesus said one warm word: "Come!" So Peter climbed over the side of the boat. He stepped down onto the water—and he did not sink! Keeping his eyes on Jesus, Peter walked across the waves toward Him. For a moment, Peter was doing the impossible, because he was trusting Jesus.

But then Peter looked away from Jesus and noticed the wind whipping all around him. He saw the big, splashing waves, and he grew afraid again. As soon as his fear took over, he began to sink down into the water. "Lord, save me!" he cried out.

And right away—not a moment later—Jesus reached out His hand and caught him. He held Peter up and gently lifted him, and together they walked back to the boat. Jesus said to him kindly, "You of little faith, why did you doubt?" Jesus was not scolding Peter. He was teaching him something to remember forever: when we keep our eyes on Jesus and trust Him, we do not need to be afraid.

When Jesus and Peter climbed into the boat, something wonderful happened. The wind stopped blowing. The waves lay down flat and quiet. The whole stormy sea grew calm and still. The disciples looked at Jesus with hearts full of awe, and they worshiped Him, saying, "You are truly the Son of God!" (Matthew 14:33). They were beginning to understand just who was in the boat with them.

Sometimes our days can feel a little like that stormy lake—when things are hard, or scary, or too big for us. We might feel like Peter, starting to sink. But here is the good news: Jesus is always near, and the moment we call out to Him, He reaches out His hand. He never lets go.

So tonight, little one, if any worry tries to follow you to bed, you can remember Peter on the water and the strong, gentle hand of Jesus catching him. You do not have to be afraid, because Jesus is right here with you, just as He was in the boat with His friends. Keep your heart turned toward Him, and let His kind voice settle over you: "Don't be afraid." You are safe, you are loved, and He is watching over you all through the night. Sweet dreams, dear one.
takeaway: When we keep our eyes on Jesus and call to Him, He reaches out and holds us, so we don't need to be afraid.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You for always being near, even when things feel scary. When I start to feel afraid, help me reach for Your hand, just like Peter did. Hold me close and give me peaceful rest tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: “Cheer up! It is I! Don’t be afraid.” — Matthew 14:27
talkAboutIt: Peter was fine while he looked at Jesus, but grew afraid when he looked at the waves. What is something that worries you that you could give to Jesus tonight?

title: The Mustard Seed
subtitle: A story about hope
storyText:
One day, Jesus sat down by the shore of the lake, and a great big crowd gathered close around Him to listen. Jesus loved to teach people about God using stories called parables. A parable is a little story that holds a big truth inside it, the way a tiny seed holds a whole plant. And on this day, Jesus told them a story about the very smallest of seeds.

"The Kingdom of Heaven," Jesus began in His gentle voice, "is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and sowed in his field" (Matthew 13:31). Have you ever seen a mustard seed? It is so very tiny that it could sit on the tip of your finger and almost disappear. You could hold a hundred of them in the palm of your hand. It is one of the littlest seeds there is.

The people leaned in to listen. Jesus told them how a man took that teeny-tiny seed and planted it in the ground. It was so small that you might think nothing much could ever come of it. But Jesus knew a wonderful secret about that little seed.

"It indeed is smaller than all seeds," Jesus said, "but when it is grown, it is greater than the herbs, and becomes a tree, so that the birds of the air come and lodge in its branches" (Matthew 13:32). Can you imagine it? That tiny, tiny seed grew and grew and grew, taller than all the other plants in the garden, until it became a great big tree with strong, leafy branches. And the birds flew down from the sky and built their nests there, resting safe and cozy among its branches.

From something so small came something so big and strong! That is what Jesus wanted His friends to understand. God can take what looks tiny and almost too small to matter, and grow it into something wonderful and beautiful—something large enough to shelter and bless many others.

It is true for love, too, and for kindness, and for faith. A small, gentle thing—a kind word, a quiet prayer, a little hope tucked inside your heart—is never too small for God. He can make it grow into something far bigger than you could ever guess. The mighty kingdom of God begins in small, quiet ways, just like the tallest tree begins with one little seed.

So you do not have to do great big things to make God happy, little one. You can offer your small kindnesses and your little hopes, and then trust God to do the growing. Even the tiniest good thing, planted with love, can grow and grow until it is something truly beautiful.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down beneath your blanket, think of that tiny mustard seed becoming a great, sheltering tree. Maybe there is a small hope in your heart—a wish, a dream, or a kind thing you want to do tomorrow. You can give it to God and trust Him to help it grow. Close your eyes, and let your heart be full of hope. The God who grows the smallest seeds is watching over you, and He will keep you safe all through the night. Sleep well, sweet child.
takeaway: God can take the smallest things—a tiny seed, a little hope, a small kindness—and grow them into something wonderful.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for showing me that even tiny things can grow big in Your hands. Help me plant little seeds of love and kindness, and trust You to make them grow. Please give me peaceful, happy rest tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: “The Kingdom of Heaven is like a grain of mustard seed.” — Matthew 13:31
talkAboutIt: A tiny mustard seed grew into a tree big enough for birds to nest in. What small, kind thing could you do tomorrow that God might grow into something big?

title: Jesus Heals the Blind Man
subtitle: A story about love
storyText:
One bright day, Jesus was walking through the streets of Jerusalem with His disciples, the friends who followed Him everywhere. As they walked, "he saw a man blind from birth" (John 9:1). This man had never once seen the blue of the sky, or the green of the hills, or the faces of the people he loved. From the very first day of his life, his world had been dark. He sat by the road and listened to the city move all around him.

Jesus' disciples looked at the man and wondered aloud why he had been born blind. People in those days often thought that sickness meant someone had done something wrong. But Jesus gently told them they had it all backward. "Neither did this man sin, nor his parents," He said, "but, that the works of God might be revealed in him" (John 9:3). This man was not a mistake. God was about to do something wonderful through him, something everyone would remember.

Then Jesus said the words that tell us who He is: "While I am in the world, I am the light of the world" (John 9:5). Imagine that — the Light of the world, standing right beside a man who had only ever known the dark.

What Jesus did next was unusual and tender. He bent down low to the ground and made a little bit of mud. Then, very gently, He spread the soft mud over the blind man's eyes. The man could not see what was happening, but he could feel kind hands touching him, hands that meant only good. Then Jesus told him, "Go, wash in the pool of Siloam" (John 9:7). The pool's name meant "Sent."

So the man went. We can picture him feeling his way along the streets, with the cool mud upon his eyes, hoping and trusting all the way to the water. He knelt at the edge of the pool of Siloam and washed his face. And the Bible says that he "went away, washed, and came back seeing" (John 9:7).

Seeing! For the very first time in his whole life, light came pouring in. Colors he had never known. The brightness of the sun, the shapes of the houses, the kind faces of the people nearby. Everything was new and beautiful. His dark world had become full of light.

His neighbors could hardly believe it. "Isn't this the same man who used to sit and beg by the road?" they asked one another. Some said yes, and some said it only looked like him. But the man kept saying, "I am the one!" They asked him again and again how it had happened, and how his eyes had been opened.

The man did not have all the answers. He could not explain everything about Jesus yet. But he knew the one thing that mattered most, and he said it simply and bravely: "One thing I do know: that though I was blind, now I see" (John 9:25). He could not deny what Jesus had done. His whole life had changed.

Jesus opened that man's eyes because He saw him, loved him, and chose him. Jesus saw a man everyone else walked past, and He stopped. He stops for us too. Sometimes we may feel like no one notices us, or that our troubles are too small to matter. But Jesus, the Light of the world, always sees, and His love can fill even the darkest, quietest places with light.

So tonight, little one, as you close your eyes and the room grows dark, you do not need to be afraid of the dark at all. The same Jesus who is the Light of the world is right here with you. He sees you, He knows your name, and He loves you more than you can imagine. Rest softly now, safe in His gentle care, all through the night.
takeaway: Jesus, the Light of the world, sees us and fills our darkest places with His love.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You that You are the Light of the world and that You always see me. When things feel dark or scary, help me remember that You are near. Help me rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "While I am in the world, I am the light of the world." — John 9:5
talkAboutIt: The man said, "Though I was blind, now I see." What is something good Jesus has helped you to see or understand?

title: The Sower and the Seeds
subtitle: A story about hope
storyText:
One day, Jesus sat down beside the Sea of Galilee, and so many people gathered to hear Him that He climbed into a little boat and pushed out a short way onto the water. From there, the gentle waves carried His voice to the great crowd waiting on the shore. Jesus loved to teach with stories about the world around them, and on this day He told them a story about a farmer and his seeds.

"Behold, a farmer went out to sow" (Matthew 13:3), Jesus began. The farmer carried a big bag of seed, and as he walked across his field, he scattered the seeds far and wide with his hand. But the seeds did not all land in the same kind of place.

Some seeds fell on the hard path where people walked. The ground there was too packed and firm for the seeds to sink in, and soon the birds came swooping down and ate them all up.

Other seeds fell on rocky ground, where there was only a thin layer of soil over the stone. Those seeds sprang up quickly, eager and green. But because the soil was so shallow, the little plants had no deep roots. When the hot sun rose, the tender shoots had nothing to hold them, and they withered away.

Still other seeds fell among thorns and weeds. The seeds began to grow, but the prickly thorns grew up taller and faster all around them, crowding them and choking them, until those little plants could not grow at all.

But then Jesus smiled and told them about the very best ground. "Others fell on good soil, and yielded fruit: some one hundred times as much, some sixty, and some thirty" (Matthew 13:8). In the soft, rich, ready soil, the seeds sent down deep roots and grew strong and tall, until the whole field was waving with grain — far, far more than the farmer had scattered. When He had finished, Jesus said, "He who has ears to hear, let him hear" (Matthew 13:9).

Later, when Jesus was alone with His friends, they asked Him what the story meant. So Jesus explained it gently. The seed, He said, is God's word — the good news of His love. And the different kinds of ground are like the different ways our hearts can listen.

The hard path is like a heart that is too busy or too closed to let God's word in, so the word is snatched away before it can grow. The rocky ground is like a heart that is happy to hear about God for a moment, but has no deep roots, so when hard days come, it gives up. The thorny ground is like a heart that lets too many worries and wants crowd in, until there is no room left for God's word to grow.

But the good soil — ah, the good soil. "What was sown on the good ground, this is he who hears the word, and understands it, who most certainly bears fruit" (Matthew 13:23). A soft, open, listening heart takes God's word deep inside, and it grows and grows into something beautiful, far more than we could ever expect.

We get to choose, every day, what kind of soil our hearts will be. We can keep our hearts soft and ready, listening for God's voice, making room for His love to take root. And when we do, God promises that good and beautiful things will grow inside us — kindness, joy, gentleness, and faith — more than we could ever imagine.

So tonight, little one, as you settle into your warm bed, you can let your heart grow soft and quiet like good soil. Let God's gentle love sink down deep inside you while you sleep. He has planted seeds of hope in your heart, and He will tend them with the greatest care, today, tonight, and all your days.
takeaway: When we keep our hearts soft and open to God's word, beautiful things grow inside us.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for planting Your love in my heart like a good seed. Help my heart stay soft and ready to listen to You. Help good things grow in me as I rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Others fell on good soil, and yielded fruit: some one hundred times as much, some sixty, and some thirty." — Matthew 13:8
talkAboutIt: Jesus said the good soil is a heart that hears God's word and lets it grow. What is one way you can keep your heart soft and ready to listen this week?

title: Mary and Martha
subtitle: A story about peace
storyText:
In a little village called Bethany, there lived two sisters named Martha and Mary. They loved each other dearly, and they shared a home together. One day, the most wonderful visitor came to their door — Jesus Himself, with His friends. Martha was so happy to welcome Him inside that she opened her home wide and began to get everything ready.

There was so very much to do! Martha wanted everything to be just right for Jesus. She bustled about, cooking and serving, fetching and carrying, making sure there was good food and a clean, comfortable place for her special guest. Her hands were busy from morning into the afternoon, and her mind was busy too, spinning with all the tasks still left to finish.

But while Martha worked and worried, her sister Mary did something different. Mary "sat at Jesus' feet, and heard his word" (Luke 10:39). She settled herself down right beside Jesus, quiet and still, and she listened to every word He said. The cooking could wait. The cleaning could wait. Right now, the most important thing in the whole world was sitting in her home, and Mary did not want to miss a single word.

After a while, Martha grew tired and a little upset. There she was, doing all the work, while Mary just sat! It did not seem fair. So Martha came to Jesus and said, "Lord, don't you care that my sister left me to serve alone? Ask her therefore to help me" (Luke 10:40).

Jesus understood Martha's heart. He loved her dearly, and He could see how hard she was working and how worried she had become. So He answered her with great gentleness, even saying her name twice, the way you might softly say the name of someone you love. "Martha, Martha," He said, "you are anxious and troubled about many things, but one thing is needed. Mary has chosen the good part, which will not be taken away from her" (Luke 10:41-42).

Jesus was not cross with Martha. He was not telling her that helping and serving were bad — those are good and kind things to do. He was teaching her something tender and important: that being with Him, listening to Him, and resting in His love is the best thing of all. Mary had chosen that good part, and Jesus would never take it away from her.

Sometimes our days can feel just like Martha's — so full and so busy, with so much to do that our hearts begin to hurry and fret. We rush from one thing to the next, and our minds spin with all our little worries. But Jesus gently reminds us, just as He reminded Martha, that there is something better than all our busyness. It is simply being near to Him, quiet and still, and letting His love settle our hearts.

We do not have to do everything. We do not have to get everything perfect. We can stop, and breathe, and rest, and choose the good part — the part that is being close to Jesus, who loves us so.

So tonight, little one, as the day grows quiet and your busy thoughts grow slow, you can be just like Mary. You can rest near to Jesus, who is always close beside you. You do not need to hurry anymore tonight. You do not need to worry about a single thing. Let your heart grow calm and peaceful, and rest softly in His love, all through the gentle night.
takeaway: The best thing of all is to be still and rest close to Jesus, who loves us.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, sometimes my heart feels busy and worried like Martha's. Help me be still and rest close to You, because You love me. Quiet my heart and help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Mary has chosen the good part, which will not be taken away from her." — Luke 10:42
talkAboutIt: Jesus told Martha that being close to Him was the good part. What is a quiet way our family can spend time being close to Jesus together?

title: The Ten Lepers
subtitle: A story about prayer
storyText:
One day, Jesus was walking along the road on His way toward Jerusalem, traveling near the lands of Samaria and Galilee. As He came near a little village, ten men saw Him coming. These ten men were very sick with a skin sickness called leprosy. In those days, people with this sickness had to live far away from everyone else, away from their homes and their families. They were lonely, and they were sad.

Because of their sickness, the ten men could not come close to Jesus. So they stood far off, and they lifted up their voices as loud as they could and called out together, "Jesus, Master, have mercy on us!" (Luke 17:13). They had heard about Jesus. They believed that He could help them, and so they cried out to Him with all their hearts.

Jesus heard them. He always hears. He looked at the ten men with great kindness, and He told them to do something that took faith. "Go and show yourselves to the priests" (Luke 17:14), He said. Now, in those days, only a priest could say that someone was truly well again. So Jesus was asking the men to start walking to the priests before anything had even changed — to trust Him, and go.

And the men did. They turned and began to walk down the road, still sick, still sore, but believing. And the Bible says that "as they went, they were cleansed" (Luke 17:14). As they walked, their skin grew well and whole again! Their sores were gone. They were healed! Can you imagine their joy, looking down at their own hands and seeing they were better at last?

All ten men were healed. But then something sad and something beautiful happened together. Nine of the men kept right on running, so excited to get home that they forgot all about the One who had healed them. But one man — and he was a Samaritan, a stranger — stopped. When he saw that he was healed, he turned around and came back. He praised God with a loud, happy voice. He fell down at Jesus' feet and said thank You, again and again.

Jesus looked at the one thankful man, and then He looked down the road where the other nine had gone. Gently He asked, "Weren't the ten cleansed? But where are the nine?" (Luke 17:17). Ten men had been healed, but only one had remembered to come back and say thank You.

Then Jesus said something kind and warm to the man kneeling before Him. "Get up, and go your way. Your faith has healed you" (Luke 17:19). The man had not only been made well in his body — his trusting, thankful heart had brought him close to Jesus, too.

This is a story about saying thank You. When good things come to us — when we are well, when we are loved, when we have food and a warm bed and people who care for us — it is the very best thing to stop and remember God, and to thank Him. Saying thank You to God is one of the sweetest kinds of prayer there is. It fills our hearts with joy, and it brings us close to the One who loves us.

So tonight, little one, before you drift off to sleep, you can be just like that one thankful man.
