You can think of all the good gifts in your day — the people who love you, the kindness you were shown, your soft pillow and your cozy blankets — and you can whisper a quiet thank You to God. He hears every grateful word, and He loves you so very much. Rest now, peaceful and thankful, all through the night.
takeaway: Saying thank You to God is one of the sweetest prayers of all.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for all the good gifts You give me every single day. Help me remember to say thank You, just like the one happy man who came back to Jesus. Help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Get up, and go your way. Your faith has healed you." — Luke 17:19
talkAboutIt: Only one of the ten men came back to say thank You. What is something good from today that you would like to thank God for?

title: Jesus in the Garden of Gethsemane
subtitle: A story about prayer
storyText:
One quiet evening, after sharing a special supper with His friends, Jesus walked with them to a peaceful garden called Gethsemane. It was a place of olive trees, with soft grass beneath them and the night sky stretching wide overhead. Jesus loved to come here to pray. But on this night, His heart was very heavy, for He knew that a hard day was coming soon.

When they reached the garden, Jesus said to most of His friends, "Sit here, while I go there and pray." Then He took three of His closest friends — Peter, James, and John — a little further with Him. Jesus felt deeply sad, and He did not want to be all alone. He told them softly, "My soul is exceedingly sorrowful, even to death. Stay here, and watch with me" (Matthew 26:38). He simply wanted His friends near while He talked to His Father.

Then Jesus went forward a little way by Himself. He knelt down low, all the way to the ground, and He prayed. He poured out everything that was in His heart to God, His Father in heaven. He did not hide His sadness or pretend to be brave. He told God exactly how He felt, the way you can tell God anything at all.

Jesus prayed, "My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass away from me; nevertheless, not what I desire, but what you desire" (Matthew 26:39). Jesus was asking, with all His heart, if there might be another way. But even in His sorrow, He trusted His Father completely. More than anything, He wanted to do what God wanted. "Not what I desire, but what you desire," He said. That is one of the bravest and most loving prayers ever prayed.

When Jesus walked back to His three friends, He found them fast asleep. The night was late, and their eyes had grown so heavy that they could not stay awake. Jesus woke them gently. He was not angry with them. He understood how tired they were. He simply said, "Watch and pray, that you don't enter into temptation. The spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak" (Matthew 26:41). He knew that praying helps us stay strong, and that talking to God gives us courage when things are hard.

Jesus went away and prayed the same prayer again, and then a third time, always trusting His Father. And do you know what happened in that garden? As Jesus prayed and poured out His heart, God gave Him peace and strength to face what was ahead. Prayer did not make the hard thing disappear, but it filled Jesus with the courage and calm He needed. He rose up ready, steady, and brave, held safe in His Father's love.

This is what prayer is for us, too. When something feels too big, or too sad, or too scary, we do not have to keep it locked up inside. We can do exactly what Jesus did. We can find a quiet place, and we can tell God everything — every worry, every sadness, every fear. And when we trust Him with it all, God gives us peace and strength, just as He gave to Jesus in the garden.

So tonight, little one, as you lie warm and safe in your bed, you can talk to God about anything at all. He is always listening, and He always cares. You can tell Him your happy things and your worried things, and then you can rest, knowing He is holding you close. Let your heart grow quiet and peaceful now, wrapped up in His gentle love, all through the night.
takeaway: When we tell God everything in prayer and trust Him, He gives us peace and strength.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that I can tell You anything, just like Jesus did in the garden. When something feels too big, help me trust You and feel Your peace. Help me rest gently tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Not what I desire, but what you desire." — Matthew 26:39
talkAboutIt: Jesus told God exactly how He felt in the garden. What is something on your heart that you would like to talk to God about tonight?

title: The Empty Tomb
subtitle: A story about hope
storyText:
It was very early in the morning, just as the sky was beginning to glow with the first soft light of a new day. Two women named Mary Magdalene and the other Mary set out together, walking quietly toward the tomb where Jesus had been laid to rest. A tomb was like a little room carved out of rock, and a great heavy stone had been rolled in front of it. The women's hearts were sad and heavy, for they loved Jesus dearly and they missed Him so.

But that morning, something wonderful was about to happen — something so full of joy that the whole world would remember it forever.

As the two women came near, the ground gave a great shake, and an angel of the Lord came down from heaven. The angel rolled away the heavy stone from the door of the tomb and sat upon it. The angel shone bright as lightning, with clothes as white as fresh snow. The women were filled with wonder and a little bit of fear at such a sight.

But the angel spoke to them in a kind and gentle voice. "Don't be afraid," the angel said, "for I know that you seek Jesus, who has been crucified. He is not here, for he has risen, just like he said. Come, see the place where the Lord was lying" (Matthew 28:5-6).

He is not here — He has risen! The two Marys looked inside, and it was true. The tomb was empty. Jesus was alive again, just as He had promised! The angel told them to hurry and bring the happy news to Jesus' friends.

So the women ran. The Bible says, "They departed quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy, and ran to bring his disciples word" (Matthew 28:8). Their feet flew along the road, their hearts pounding with the most amazing joy. The sadness had turned into gladness. The dark night was over, and morning had truly come.

And then, as they were running, the most wonderful thing of all happened. Jesus Himself met them on the road! He was standing right there, alive, smiling at them with so much love. "Rejoice!" He said — which means, "Be happy! Be glad!" The women could hardly believe their eyes. They came close and took hold of His feet and worshiped Him, their hearts overflowing.

Jesus spoke to them tenderly. "Don't be afraid," He said. "Go tell my brothers that they should go into Galilee, and there they will see me" (Matthew 28:10). He was alive, and He wanted all His friends to know. Soon they would all see Him again and rejoice together.

This is the happiest story in the whole Bible. Jesus, who loves us with all His heart, is alive! Because He rose from the tomb, we know that love is stronger than anything sad, and that God's joy and hope shine brighter than the darkest night. No matter how sad a thing may seem, with God, joy always comes in the morning.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down beneath your covers, you can hold this happy truth close to your heart. Jesus is alive, and He loves you so. There is nothing to be afraid of, for He is always near. Just as morning followed that quiet, dark night long ago, a bright new morning is coming for you, too. Rest now in His great joy and His gentle love, sleeping peacefully all through the night.
takeaway: Jesus is alive, and His love and joy are stronger than anything sad.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You that You are alive and that You love me so much. Thank You that Your joy is stronger than anything sad. Fill my heart with Your happiness and help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "He is not here, for he has risen, just like he said." — Matthew 28:6
talkAboutIt: The women's sadness turned into great joy when they found Jesus was alive. What is something that fills your heart with joy?

title: Peter Walks on Water
subtitle: A story about courage
storyText:
On a windy night long ago, the disciples were out on the Sea of Galilee in their boat. Jesus had stayed behind on the shore to pray, sending His friends ahead across the water. But the wind began to blow harder and harder, and the waves rose up against the little boat. The disciples rowed and rowed, but they could not reach the other side.

It was very late — the darkest part of the night, long before morning. And then, across the rolling water, the disciples saw something they could hardly believe. Someone was coming toward them, walking right on top of the sea. "In the fourth watch of the night, Jesus came to them, walking on the sea" (Matthew 14:25).

The disciples were frightened. They did not understand how anyone could walk on water, and they cried out, "It's a ghost!" But Jesus called to them in a warm and steady voice, the voice they knew and loved. "Cheer up! It is I! Don't be afraid" (Matthew 14:27).

When Peter heard that voice, his fear began to melt away, and a brave hope grew in his heart. Peter called out across the waves, "Lord, if it is you, command me to come to you on the waters" (Matthew 14:28).

And Jesus said one gentle word: "Come!"

So Peter did something wonderful. He climbed over the side of the boat and stepped down onto the water. And the water held him! With his eyes fixed on Jesus, Peter began to walk — one step, then another — out on the wide, rolling sea, going to meet his friend.

But then Peter noticed the wind whipping around him and the great waves rising high. He looked away from Jesus and down at the storm, and fear filled his heart again. As soon as he was afraid, he began to sink down into the water. "Lord, save me!" Peter cried.

And here is the most beautiful part of all. Jesus did not wait. He did not scold. The Bible says, "Immediately Jesus stretched out his hand, took hold of him" (Matthew 14:31). Right away, Jesus caught Peter and lifted him up, holding him safe above the waves. Then Jesus asked him softly, "Why did you doubt?" — not to make Peter feel bad, but to remind him that he never had to face the storm alone.

Together they climbed back into the boat, and at once the wind grew quiet and still. The disciples looked at Jesus with wonder, and they worshiped Him, saying, "Truly you are the Son of God." They were beginning to see how mighty and how loving He really was.

Peter learned something that night that he would remember forever. Being brave does not mean you never feel afraid. Being brave means keeping your eyes on Jesus, even when the waves are tall. And when fear creeps in, as it sometimes will, you can always call out, "Lord, save me!" — and His hand is already reaching for you. He will never let you sink.

So tonight, little one, as you settle under your warm blanket, you can let every worry go quiet, the way the wind went quiet for Peter. If something feels too big or a little bit scary, you can whisper to Jesus, and He will hear you. His strong, gentle hand is holding you right now, and He will hold you safe all through the night. Sleep well, little one. You are never, ever alone.
takeaway: When we keep our eyes on Jesus, He reaches out and holds us safe, even in the storm.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You that You are always near, even when I feel afraid. When I am scared, help me keep my eyes on You and call to You for help. Hold me safe and help me sleep peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Cheer up! It is I! Don't be afraid." — Matthew 14:27
talkAboutIt: Jesus reached out and caught Peter the very moment he called for help. What is something that feels a little scary that you can ask Jesus to help you with?

title: The Widow's Offering
subtitle: A story about kindness
storyText:
One day, Jesus was in the temple in Jerusalem, the great house of worship where people came to pray and to give their gifts to God. Near one of the courtyards stood the temple treasury, where people dropped their offerings into trumpet-shaped boxes made of brass. Jesus sat down across from the treasury, in no hurry at all, and quietly watched the people come and go.

Many wealthy people walked by and gave their gifts. The Bible says, "Jesus sat down opposite the treasury, and saw how the multitude cast money into the treasury. Many who were rich cast in much" (Mark 12:41). Their coins clinked and rang as they poured into the boxes, handful after handful. Some of them may have wanted everyone to notice how much they were giving.

Then a poor widow came along. A widow was a woman whose husband had died, and in those days a widow often had very little money and no one to provide for her. This woman was one of the poorest people there. She did not have handfuls of gold or silver. She had only two tiny copper coins, the smallest coins of all, worth almost nothing at all. "A poor widow came, and she cast in two small brass coins, which equal a quadrans coin" (Mark 12:42).

Quietly, without anyone watching, she dropped both of her little coins into the treasury. It was not much money — but it was every bit she had. She did not keep one coin back for herself. She gave it all, trusting God to take care of her.

Most people in the temple never even noticed. But Jesus noticed. He sees what others miss, and He looks straight at the heart. Jesus called His disciples close and said to them, "Most certainly I tell you, this poor widow gave more than all those who are giving into the treasury, for they all gave out of their abundance, but she, out of her poverty, gave all that she had to live on" (Mark 12:43-44).

What a surprising thing to say! The rich people had given far more coins. But Jesus measured the gifts a different way. The wealthy gave from the extra they had left over, money they would never miss. The widow gave from her very little, holding nothing back. In God's eyes, her two small coins were the greatest gift of all, because she gave with all her heart and all her trust.

Jesus was teaching His friends, and He is teaching us, that God does not measure our gifts by how big they look. He looks at the love behind them. A small thing given with a whole heart is precious to God. You do not have to be rich or grown-up or important to give something beautiful to God. A kind word, a shared toy, a helping hand, a quiet prayer — when you give it with love, God sees it and treasures it.

And just as the widow trusted God with all she had, you can trust Him too. The same God who watched over that dear woman watches over you. He knows exactly what you need, and He will always take care of you.

So tonight, little one, as your head rests softly on your pillow, you can remember that God sees every loving thing you do, even the small and quiet ones that no one else notices. Your kindness is precious to Him. Tomorrow you can give a little gift of love to someone — but for now, rest. You are seen, you are loved, and you are safe in the care of the God who watches over you all night long.
takeaway: God treasures even the smallest gift when it is given with a whole and loving heart.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You that You see every loving thing I do, even the small ones. Help me give my kindness freely and trust You to take care of me. Help me rest peacefully tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "This poor widow gave more than all those who are giving into the treasury." — Mark 12:43
talkAboutIt: The widow gave her two small coins with all her heart, and Jesus said it was the greatest gift. What is one small, loving thing you could give to someone tomorrow?

title: Jesus and the Woman at the Well
subtitle: A story about love
storyText:
One hot afternoon, Jesus was walking through a land called Samaria with His disciples. The sun was high and the road was dusty, and Jesus grew tired from the long journey. He came to a famous old well — Jacob's well, it was called — and He sat down beside it to rest while His friends went into the nearby town to buy some food.

The sun was at its hottest, the time of day when most people stayed inside and out of the heat. But just then, a woman of the town came alone to the well to draw water. Usually women came together in the cool of the morning or evening, chatting with their friends. This woman came by herself, in the middle of the day, because she felt that the others did not want her near. She carried a heavy heart along with her water jar.

When she arrived, Jesus did something unexpected. In those days, the people of Samaria and the people Jesus came from did not usually speak to one another, and a teacher would rarely speak to a woman he did not know. But Jesus looked at her kindly and said, "Give me a drink" (John 4:7).

The woman was surprised. "Why are you asking me for water?" she said. But Jesus spoke to her gently, as if she truly mattered — because to Him, she did. Then He told her about a gift far greater than the water in the well. "Everyone who drinks of this water will thirst again," He said, "but whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never thirst again; but the water that I will give him will become in him a well of water springing up to eternal life" (John 4:13-14).

The woman did not quite understand yet. "Sir, give me this water," she said, "so that I will never be thirsty again." Jesus was speaking of a thirst deeper than the kind water can quench — the thirst in our hearts to be known and loved by God. And as they talked, the woman began to realize something amazing. This kind stranger seemed to know all about her life, even the parts she felt ashamed of. Yet He did not turn away from her. He did not scold her. He simply kept speaking to her with love.

Then she wondered aloud whether the long-promised Messiah, the Savior God had promised to send, might one day come and explain everything. And Jesus said the most wonderful words of all: "I am he, the one who speaks to you" (John 4:26). The Savior the whole world had waited for was sitting right beside her at the well, talking with her, choosing her, loving her.

The woman was so full of joy that she left her water jar right there and ran back into the town. "Come, see a man who told me everything that I did," she called out to everyone. "Could this be the Christ?" Because of her happy news, many people came out to meet Jesus, and they came to believe in Him too. The woman who once felt so alone became the one who brought a whole town to the Savior.

Jesus showed her, and He shows us, that He sees each one of us completely. He knows everything about us — every happy thing and every hard thing — and He loves us still. We never have to hide from Him or feel too small or alone. His love is like that living water, springing up fresh and full and never running out.

So tonight, little one, as you snuggle down beneath the covers, you can rest in this gentle truth: Jesus sees you and knows you and loves you, just as you are. You are never alone, and you are never forgotten. Let His love fill your heart like cool, fresh water, and sleep peacefully, dearly loved, all through the quiet night.
takeaway: Jesus knows us completely and loves us still, offering a living love that never runs dry.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You that You see me and know me and love me just as I am. Fill my heart with Your love like cool, fresh water. Help me sleep peacefully, knowing I am never alone. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never thirst again." — John 4:14
talkAboutIt: Jesus knew everything about the woman at the well and loved her anyway. How does it feel to know that Jesus sees you and loves you exactly as you are?

title: The Talents
subtitle: A story about trust
storyText:
Jesus loved to teach with stories, and one day He told a story about a man who was going away on a long journey. Before he left, this master called his servants together, because he wanted to trust them with something very valuable while he was gone.

To his servants the master gave talents — talents were large amounts of money in those days. But he did not give them all the same. The Bible says, "To one he gave five talents, to another two, to another one; to each according to his own ability" (Matthew 25:15). He gave to each servant just what he knew that servant could handle. Then the master went away on his journey, trusting them to take good care of what was his.

The first servant, who had been given five talents, went right to work. He used the money wisely and faithfully, and over time it grew, until he had five more — ten talents in all! The second servant, who had been given two talents, worked faithfully as well, and his two grew into four. Both of them used what their master had given them, and both of them brought back even more.

But the third servant, who had been given one talent, was afraid. "What if I lose it?" he worried. So instead of using his gift, he dug a hole in the ground and buried the talent there. He hid it away in the dark and did nothing with it at all.

After a long time, the master came home, and he gathered his servants to see what they had done. The first servant stepped forward joyfully. "Master, you gave me five talents, and look — I have gained five more!" The master was so pleased. He said the words every faithful servant longs to hear: "Well done, good and faithful servant. You have been faithful over a few things, I will set you over many things. Enter into the joy of your lord" (Matthew 25:21).

Then the second servant came. "Master, you gave me two talents, and I have gained two more." And the master was just as delighted with him. "Well done, good and faithful servant!" he said, for this servant had been faithful too, even though he had been given less.

At last the third servant came, holding out his one buried talent. "Master, I was afraid, so I hid your talent in the ground. Here it is, just as you gave it to me." The master was sad, not because the servant had little, but because he had let his fear keep him from using the good gift he had been given at all.

Jesus told this story to teach us something tender and true. God has given each of us special gifts — and not one of us is left out. To some He gives one kind of gift and to others another, "to each according to his own ability." He does not ask us to be the best or to do the most. He simply asks us to faithfully use the good things He has placed in us, with trust in Him.

Maybe your gift is a kind heart, or a cheerful song, or quick hands for helping, or a mind that loves to learn. Whatever it is, it is a treasure from God, and He delights when you use it with love instead of hiding it away in fear. You do not have to be afraid. The God who gave you your gifts is right beside you, helping you, and one day He longs to say to you, "Well done."

So tonight, little one, as your eyes grow heavy and your blanket feels warm, you can thank God for the special gifts He has tucked inside you. There is no need to be afraid of tomorrow. Rest now, safe and loved, and dream of all the good and beautiful things you will share with the world when morning comes. Sleep well, faithful little one.
takeaway: God gives each of us special gifts and delights when we use them faithfully and without fear.
bedtimePrayer: Dear God, thank You for the special gifts You have given me. Help me not to be afraid, but to use them with love to bless others. Help me rest peacefully tonight, safe in Your care. Amen.
memoryVerse: "Well done, good and faithful servant." — Matthew 25:21
talkAboutIt: God gave each servant a gift to use. What is a special gift God has given you that you could share with someone tomorrow?

title: Jesus Washes the Disciples' Feet
subtitle: A story about kindness
storyText:
It was the evening before a very special day. Jesus had gathered with His twelve disciples to share a quiet supper together in an upstairs room. Jesus knew that His time on earth was growing short, and His heart was full of love for His friends. The Bible says it gently and beautifully: "having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end" (John 13:1).

In those days, people walked everywhere in open sandals along dusty roads, so their feet became dirty and tired. Whenever guests arrived at a home, the lowest servant of the household would kneel down and wash the dust from everyone's feet. It was humble work, the kind of job no one wanted to do.

That evening, there was no servant in the room — only Jesus and His friends. And so, in the middle of the meal, Jesus rose from the table and did something that made everyone go quiet. He took off His outer robe, wrapped a towel around His waist just as a servant would, and poured water into a basin. Then the Teacher, the Lord Himself, knelt down on the floor and began to wash His disciples' feet, one by one, drying them carefully with the towel.

When Jesus came to Peter, Peter pulled his feet back, surprised and uncomfortable. "Lord, are You going to wash my feet?" he said. "You are my Teacher! I should be washing Your feet, not the other way around." But Jesus answered him kindly, "You don't understand now what I am doing, but afterward you will understand." And so Peter let Jesus wash his feet too.

When Jesus had finished, He put His robe back on and sat down again with His friends. "Do you understand what I have done to you?" He asked them gently. "You call me Teacher and Lord. And you say so correctly, for so I am." Then Jesus explained the beautiful lesson hidden in the warm water and the towel: "If I then, the Lord and the Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet. For I have given you an example, that you also should do as I have done to you" (John 13:14-15).

Jesus was showing His friends what real love looks like. The greatest one in the room had become like a servant, kneeling low to care for the others. Jesus was not too important or too proud to do humble, gentle work for the people He loved. And He wanted His friends to live the very same way — quietly serving and caring for one another with glad hearts.

This is what it means to follow Jesus. It is not about being the biggest or the most important. It is about loving others enough to help them, even in the small and humble things. Helping someone clean up. Sharing without being asked. Doing a kind chore with a cheerful heart. Each loving act, however small, shines with the same love Jesus showed when He knelt down with the towel and the basin.

And remember — Jesus did not only teach this love, He gave it to you first. He loves you all the way to the end. When you serve others with a happy heart, you are sharing the very love He poured out on His friends that quiet evening long ago.

So tonight, little one, as you curl up snug and warm, you can think of Jesus kneeling down to wash His friends' feet, loving them so tenderly. Tomorrow there will be little ways to share that love — a helping hand, a kind word, a gentle gift of care. But for now, simply rest. You are deeply loved by the One who loves to the very end, and He is watching over you all through the peaceful night.
takeaway: Jesus showed us that real love kneels down to serve others, even in the small and humble things.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You for loving Your friends so much that You washed their feet. Help me love others by serving them with a happy heart. Help me rest peacefully tonight in Your great love. Amen.
memoryVerse: "I have given you an example, that you also should do as I have done to you." — John 13:15
talkAboutIt: Jesus served His friends by washing their feet. What is a humble, helpful thing you could do for someone in your family tomorrow?

title: The Light of the World
subtitle: A story about peace
storyText:
One day, Jesus was teaching in the temple in Jerusalem, where great crowds of people had gathered to listen. It was during a special feast, a happy festival when the temple courtyards were lit up at night by enormous golden lampstands. Their flames glowed so brightly that they seemed to light up the whole city, and everyone remembered how, long ago, God had led His people through the dark wilderness with a bright pillar of fire that never went out.

In that glowing place, with the memory of that ancient light all around them, Jesus said something wonderful about Himself. He spoke to the people and told them, "I am the light of the world. He who follows me will not walk in the darkness, but will have the light of life" (John 8:12).

What did Jesus mean? Think for a moment about darkness. When a room is very dark, it can feel a little scary. You cannot see where to go. You might bump into things, or feel lost, or feel alone. But the moment a light is switched on, everything changes. You can see the way ahead. You can see the warm and friendly things around you. The fear melts away, and you feel safe again.

Jesus was saying that He is like that light, but even better — a light for our whole lives. When we follow Jesus and trust Him, He shines into our hearts and chases the darkness away. He shows us which way to go. He helps us know what is good and true and kind. And no matter how dark the night may seem, His light never, ever goes out.

That is why we never have to be afraid of the dark, and never have to feel lost or lonely. Wherever we go, Jesus, the light of the world, goes with us, shining gently along our path.

And here is something else just as beautiful. Jesus does not only want to shine on us — He wants to make us shine too! He once told His followers, "You are the light of the world. A city located on a hill can't be hidden" (Matthew 5:14). He said it this way: "Even so, let your light shine before men; that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father who is in heaven" (Matthew 5:16).

When you are kind, your light shines. When you share, or help, or speak gentle words, or love someone who is sad, a little light glows out of you into the world. Every good and loving thing you do is like a small candle pushing back the dark — and it points everyone who sees it back to God, who is the giver of all light. You do not have to be big or grown-up to shine. Even the littlest light makes the darkness flee.

So tonight, little one, even as the room grows dim and your eyelids grow heavy, you can remember that Jesus, the light of the world, is right here with you. His warm and peaceful light surrounds you, keeping you safe and never going out, all through the night. There is nothing to fear in the dark, because the One who made the light is watching over you.

Rest now, sweet child, wrapped up snug and warm in His gentle glow. And when morning comes, you can carry a little of that light back out into the world — a smile, a kindness, a glimmer of love. Sleep peacefully, little light of the world, safe and loved in the keeping of God.
takeaway: Jesus is the light who guides us, keeps us safe, and makes us shine with His love for others.
bedtimePrayer: Dear Jesus, thank You for being the light of the world and shining away every fear. Fill my heart with Your peaceful light, and help me shine kindness to others. Help me sleep safely in Your glow tonight. Amen.
memoryVerse: "I am the light of the world. He who follows me will not walk in the darkness, but will have the light of life." — John 8:12
talkAboutIt: Jesus said His followers are lights in the world. What is one kind thing you could do tomorrow to let your light shine for someone?
